alphabet.title: Alpha (Α α)
alphabet.subtitle: The first letter — Beginning & Eternity

content.name: Alpha
content.fun_fact: Jesus declares I am the Alpha and the Omega in Revelation 1:8 and 22:13. By using the first letter of the Greek alphabet He claims to be the source and origin of all things.

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Α?
What is the numeric value of Alpha?
In Revelation, Jesus calls Himself the Alpha and the ___.

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Aleph (ox head 𐤀) — represents strength, leadership, and new beginnings
content.pronunciation: ah, as in father

verse_connections.note: Jesus uses the first Greek letter to declare He is the eternal origin of all creation
verse_connections.text: I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the ending, saith the Lord

verse_connections.note: The final self-declaration of Christ — He encompasses all of reality from Α to Ω
verse_connections.text: I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end, the first and the last

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: ah, as in father
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Aleph 𐤀 — ox head, symbolising strength and the first/chief

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son
The angel of the Lord appeared unto him in a dream

alphabet.title: Beta (Β β)
alphabet.subtitle: The second letter — House & Dwelling

content.name: Beta
content.fun_fact: Bethlehem (Βηθλεέμ) means house of bread — Beth plus lehem. The eternal Word of Life was born in the House of Bread. Our word alphabet itself comes from Alpha + Beta!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Β?
The word alphabet comes from which two letters?
Bethlehem comes from Beth (house) and lehem (bread). What does it mean?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Beth (house 𐤁) — represents dwelling, family, and the household of God
content.pronunciation: b, as in boy

verse_connections.note: Bethlehem — house of bread. The Bread of Life was born in the house whose very name proclaimed Him
verse_connections.text: Now when Jesus was born in Bethlehem of Judaea in the days of Herod the king

verse_connections.note: Jesus, born in the house of bread, declares Himself to be the living bread come down from heaven
verse_connections.text: I am the bread of life: he that cometh to me shall never hunger

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: b, as in boy
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Beth 𐤁 — house, symbolising dwelling and family

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Thy kingdom come, Thy will be done in earth, as it is in heaven
Baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost

alphabet.title: Gamma (Γ γ)
alphabet.subtitle: The third letter — Scripture & Knowledge

content.name: Gamma
content.fun_fact: γραφή (graphē) means Scripture or writing. All γραφή is given by inspiration of God (2 Tim 3:16). Our words graphic, geography, and autograph all come from this Greek root!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Γ?
What does γραφή (graphē) mean?
Before which letters does Gamma produce an ng sound?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Gimel (camel 𐤂) — represents journeying and carrying burdens
content.pronunciation: g as in go; sounds like ng before κ, γ, ξ, χ (e.g. ἄγγελος = angelos)

verse_connections.note: The word scripture here is γραφή — every NT book is a divinely inspired γραφή
verse_connections.text: All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction

verse_connections.note: Jesus commands us to search the γραφαί (scriptures). They all point to Him
verse_connections.text: Search the scriptures; for in them ye think ye have eternal life: and they are they which testify of me

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: g as in go; ng before κ γ ξ χ
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Gimel 𐤂 — camel, symbolising journeying and carrying

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And this is life eternal, that they might know thee the only true God
But these are written, that ye might believe that Jesus is the Christ

alphabet.title: Delta (Δ δ)
alphabet.subtitle: The fourth letter — Power & Doorways

content.name: Delta
content.fun_fact: δύναμις (dynamis) means power and is the root of our word dynamite. Paul declared the gospel is the δύναμις of God unto salvation (Rom 1:16). The same power that raised Christ from the dead is available to every believer!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Δ?
What does δύναμις (dynamis) mean — and what English word comes from it?
What is the numeric value of Delta?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Daleth (door 𐤃) — represents a doorway, a threshold, a new path opening
content.pronunciation: d, as in dog

verse_connections.note: Power here is δύναμις — the same root as dynamite. The gospel carries explosive divine power
verse_connections.text: For I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ: for it is the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth

verse_connections.note: Power again is δύναμις — the Holy Spirit brings transforming divine power (dynamis) into our lives
verse_connections.text: But ye shall receive power, after that the Holy Ghost is come upon you: and ye shall be witnesses unto me

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: d, as in dog
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Daleth 𐤃 — door, symbolising a gateway and new path

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And now, O Father, glorify thou me with thine own self
For therein is the righteousness of God revealed from faith to faith

alphabet.title: Epsilon (Ε ε)
alphabet.subtitle: The fifth letter — The short E, Good News

content.name: Epsilon
content.fun_fact: εὐαγγέλιον (euangelion) = gospel / good news, and it starts with Epsilon! Eu means good, angelion means message. The word evangelist, evangelical, and evangelism all come from this Epsilon word.

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Ε?
εὐαγγέλιον (euangelion) means good news / gospel. What does eu mean?
Epsilon is always a SHORT sound. Which letter is the LONG e in Greek?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician He (window 𐤄) — represents breath, light coming through, revelation
content.pronunciation: SHORT e, as in met (never the long ee sound — that is Eta η)

verse_connections.note: Gospel here is εὐαγγέλιον — the good message that begins with Epsilon
verse_connections.text: The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand: repent ye, and believe the gospel

verse_connections.note: Paul stakes everything on the εὐαγγέλιον — the good news of Christ crucified and risen
verse_connections.text: Moreover, brethren, I declare unto you the gospel which I preached unto you, which also ye have received

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: SHORT e as in met (never long ee)
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician He 𐤄 — window, symbolising light and revelation entering

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Peace I leave with you, my peace I give unto you
Upon this rock I will build my church

alphabet.title: Zeta (Ζ ζ)
alphabet.subtitle: The sixth letter — Life & Vitality

content.name: Zeta
content.fun_fact: ζωή (zōē) means life — specifically eternal and spiritual life. Jesus said I am the way, the truth, and the ζωή (John 14:6). There are two words for life in Greek: bios (biological/physical life) and zōē (spiritual, God-quality life). Jesus offers zōē!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Ζ?
What does ζωή (zōē) mean?
Greek has two words for life. Zōē means spiritual life. What does bios mean?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Zayin (weapon 𐤆) — represents sharpness, the cutting edge of truth
content.pronunciation: z as in zone (in Koine/NT Greek). Classical Greek said dz as in adze

verse_connections.note: Life here is ζωή — not mere biological existence but the eternal, divine life that only Christ can give
verse_connections.text: I am the way, the truth, and the life: no man cometh unto the Father, but by me

verse_connections.note: Again ζωή — Jesus came to give us God-quality life in overflowing abundance
verse_connections.text: I am come that they might have life, and that they might have it more abundantly

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: z as in zone (Koine); dz classical
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Zayin 𐤆 — weapon, symbolising sharpness and spiritual warfare

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: I am the way, the truth, and the life
But seek ye first the kingdom of God, and his righteousness

alphabet.title: Eta (Η η)
alphabet.subtitle: The seventh letter — The long E, New Beginnings

content.name: Eta
content.fun_fact: Eta equals 8 in Greek numerals. Eight is the number of new beginnings in Scripture: Noah saved 8 souls through the flood; circumcision was performed on the 8th day. After the 7th day (Sabbath rest) comes the 8th — new creation!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Η?
What is the numeric value of Eta — also significant as the number of new beginnings?
Eta is the LONG e sound. Epsilon is the SHORT e. How should you pronounce Eta?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Heth (fence / enclosed courtyard 𐤇) — represents a protected, sacred space
content.pronunciation: LONG ay as in day or hey — hold the sound longer than Epsilon

verse_connections.note: Eight (Eta = 8) souls saved — a picture of resurrection and new beginning through the waters of baptism
verse_connections.text: Which sometime were disobedient, when once the longsuffering of God waited in the days of Noah, while the ark was a preparing, wherein few, that is, eight souls were saved by water

verse_connections.note: New creation — the eighth-day reality. In Christ we step into eternal newness
verse_connections.text: Therefore if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature: old things are passed away; behold, all things are become new

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: LONG ay as in day (hold the sound)
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Heth 𐤇 — fence/courtyard, enclosed and protected space

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Sufficient unto the day is the evil thereof
He maketh his sun to rise on the evil and on the good

alphabet.title: Theta (Θ θ)
alphabet.subtitle: The eighth letter — The God Letter

content.name: Theta
content.fun_fact: θεός (theos) means God and appears 1,317 times in the NT. It is the signature Theta word. Ancient NT scribes abbreviated sacred names (nomina sacra) — θεός was written as ΘΣ with a line over it. When you see Θ, think of God.

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Θ?
θεός (theos) means God. Which English word-family comes directly from theos?
How should you pronounce Theta (Θ)?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Teth (wheel or coiled serpent 𐤈) — represents completeness and turning
content.pronunciation: th as in think or theology (never the voiced th of the word the)

verse_connections.note: θεός appears twice in this verse — the most profound declaration about divine nature in all of Scripture
verse_connections.text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God

verse_connections.note: θεός ἀγάπη ἐστίν — God IS love. The nature of God expressed in four Greek words
verse_connections.text: He that loveth not knoweth not God; for God is love

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: th as in think (never as in the)
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Teth 𐤈 — wheel or coiled rope, symbolising completeness

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God
For the wages of sin is death; but the gift of God is eternal life

alphabet.title: Iota (Ι ι)
alphabet.subtitle: The ninth letter — The smallest, yet eternal

content.name: Iota
content.fun_fact: Jesus said not one jot (ἰῶτα) shall pass from the law until all is fulfilled (Matt 5:18 KJV). Iota is the SMALLEST Greek letter — just a tiny stroke — yet Jesus said every single stroke of Scripture matters eternally. Not one iota will be forgotten!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Ι?
Jesus said not one ___ (the smallest Greek letter) shall pass from the law (Matt 5:18).
What is the numeric value of Iota?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Yod (hand 𐤉) — represents work, deed, and divine action. Yod is also the first letter of God's name YHWH!
content.pronunciation: ee as in feet (always the long ee sound)

verse_connections.note: Jot is ἰῶτα — iota. Jesus guarantees the permanence of every stroke of Scripture. Not one iota will fail
verse_connections.text: For verily I say unto you, Till heaven and earth pass, one jot or one tittle shall in no wise pass from the law, till all be fulfilled

verse_connections.note: The tiniest mark in Scripture is upheld by the eternal word of Christ — not one iota of God's promise fails
verse_connections.text: And it is easier for heaven and earth to pass, than one tittle of the law to fail

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: ee as in feet (always the long ee)
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Yod 𐤉 — hand, symbolising work and divine action

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Thou shalt call his name JESUS: for he shall save his people from their sins
Make not my Father's house an house of merchandise

alphabet.title: Kappa (Κ κ)
alphabet.subtitle: The tenth letter — Lord & Authority

content.name: Kappa
content.fun_fact: κύριος (kyrios) means Lord and is used 748 times in the NT. The earliest Christian confession was κύριος Ἰησοῦς — Jesus is Lord (Rom 10:9). Every knee will bow and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is κύριος (Phil 2:11)!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Κ?
What does κύριος (kyrios) mean?
What is the earliest recorded Christian confession involving a Kappa word?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Kaph (open palm of hand 𐤊) — represents receiving, grasping, and extending grace
content.pronunciation: k as in kite (never silent, always crisp)

verse_connections.note: Lord here is κύριος — the confession of Kappa. This declaration of Christ's lordship brings salvation
verse_connections.text: That if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in thine heart that God hath raised him from the dead, thou shalt be saved

verse_connections.note: κύριος again — ultimately every created being will acknowledge the Lordship of Jesus Christ
verse_connections.text: And that every tongue should confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: k as in kite (crisp, never silent)
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Kaph 𐤊 — open palm, symbolising receiving and extending grace

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: That if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus
Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart

alphabet.title: Lambda (Λ λ)
alphabet.subtitle: The eleventh letter — The Word & Reason

content.name: Lambda
content.fun_fact: λόγος (logos) means Word, Reason, or Divine Expression. John opens his Gospel: In the beginning was the λόγος. Greek philosophers saw logos as the ordering reason behind the universe. John declares: that Logos is a Person — Jesus Christ — and He became flesh!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Λ?
What does λόγος (logos) mean?
John 1:1 says In the beginning was the logos. John 1:14 tells us what happened to the logos. What was it?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Lamed (ox goad 𐤋) — represents direction, teaching, and guiding toward a goal
content.pronunciation: l as in love (clear, liquid sound)

verse_connections.note: Word is λόγος — the eternal divine reason and expression. Lambda launches the most profound verse about Christ in all of Scripture
verse_connections.text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God

verse_connections.note: The λόγος became σάρξ (flesh) — the eternal Word took on human nature. Lambda carries the weight of the Incarnation
verse_connections.text: And the Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us, and we beheld his glory

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: l as in love (clear liquid sound)
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Lamed 𐤋 — ox goad, symbolising direction and teaching

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God
He shall save his people from their sins

alphabet.title: Mu (Μ μ)
alphabet.subtitle: The twelfth letter — Discipleship & Learning

content.name: Mu
content.fun_fact: μαθητής (mathētēs) means disciple or learner and appears 261 times in the Gospels and Acts. Jesus calls us all to be μαθηταί — not just believers but active learners and followers. The word mathematics also comes from the same root (máthēma = that which is learned)!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Μ?
What does μαθητής (mathētēs) mean?
The Great Commission says to make disciples of all nations. The word disciple is μαθητής. What did Jesus command His disciples to do?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Mem (water 𐤌) — represents flow, cleansing, and revelation flowing like water
content.pronunciation: m as in mother (simple nasal sound)

verse_connections.note: Teach all nations is literally make disciples (μαθητεύσατε) — Mu's great commission word. The church's core calling
verse_connections.text: Go ye therefore, and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost

verse_connections.note: Disciples (μαθηταί) are defined by continuing in Christ's word — learning, abiding, and obeying
verse_connections.text: If ye continue in my word, then are ye my disciples indeed

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: m as in mother
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Mem 𐤌 — water, symbolising flow and cleansing revelation

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Go ye therefore, and teach all nations
The same came for a witness, to bear witness of the Light

alphabet.title: Nu (Ν ν)
alphabet.subtitle: The thirteenth letter — The hidden N

content.name: Nu
content.fun_fact: The Phoenician letter Nun means fish. The early church adopted ΙΧΘΥΣ (ichthys, fish) as their secret symbol: Ἰησοῦς Χριστός Θεοῦ Υἱὸς Σωτήρ — Jesus Christ, Son of God, Savior. Every letter of that acronym is in this alphabet! Also: νόμος (nomos) = law appears 194 times in the NT.

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Ν?
WARNING: Lowercase ν looks like English v, but what sound does it make?

options: A secret acronym for Jesus Christ Son of God Savior
practice.question: The Phoenician ancestor of Nu meant fish. What did early Christians use the fish symbol (ΙΧΘΥΣ) for?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Nun (fish 𐤍) — represents life, movement, and abundance. Early Christians used the fish as their secret symbol!
content.pronunciation: n as in now — CAUTION: lowercase ν looks like English v but is always the n sound!

verse_connections.note: Jesus calls fishermen using imagery tied to Nu's Phoenician ancestor (Nun = fish). The disciples become fishers of souls
verse_connections.text: And he saith unto them, Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men

verse_connections.note: The miraculous catch of fish — a sign of the gospel harvest. Nu's fish heritage points to the abundance of souls brought into the kingdom
verse_connections.text: Simon Peter went up, and drew the net to land full of great fishes, an hundred and fifty and three

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: n as in now — NEVER v! lowercase ν looks like v but sounds like n
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Nun 𐤍 — fish, symbolising life and movement

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Do we then make void the law through faith? God forbid: yea, we establish the law
Know ye not that ye are the temple of God?

alphabet.title: Xi (Ξ ξ)
alphabet.subtitle: The fourteenth letter — The Cross & the Wood

content.name: Xi
content.fun_fact: ξύλον (xylon) means wood or tree and is used in Acts 5:30 for the cross: whom ye slew and hanged on a tree (ξύλον). Paul quotes Deuteronomy in Galatians 3:13: Cursed is every one that hangeth on a tree (ξύλον). A common piece of wood became the instrument of our eternal salvation!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Ξ?
What does ξύλον (xylon) mean — used for the cross in Acts 5:30?
How do you pronounce Xi?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Samekh (support / pillar 𐤎) — represents sustaining, upholding, the support that holds everything
content.pronunciation: ks blended together as in fox or tax (one sound, not two separate letters)

verse_connections.note: Tree here is ξύλον — the Xi word. The cross was a piece of wood (xylon), yet on it hung the salvation of the world
verse_connections.text: The God of our fathers raised up Jesus, whom ye slew and hanged on a tree

verse_connections.note: Tree again is ξύλον. Christ took the curse (hanging on ξύλον) to break our curse — the greatest exchange in history
verse_connections.text: Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law, being made a curse for us: for it is written, Cursed is every one that hangeth on a tree

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: ks blended as in fox (one sound)
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Samekh 𐤎 — support/pillar, symbolising upholding

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: The God of our fathers raised up Jesus, whom ye slew and hanged on a tree
I was a stranger, and ye took me in

alphabet.title: Omicron (Ο ο)
alphabet.subtitle: The fifteenth letter — The small O

content.name: Omicron
content.fun_fact: Omicron literally means small O in Greek (ὂ μικρόν). It contrasts with Omega which means great O (ὦ μέγα). Both were originally one sound but Greek later distinguished short and long. οὐρανός (ouranos) means heaven and appears 284 times in the NT — every time someone prays Our Father in heaven, they use an Omicron word!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Ο?
Omicron (Ο) is always a SHORT o. Which letter is the LONG o in Greek?
What does οὐρανός (ouranos) mean?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Ayin (eye 𐤏) — represents seeing, insight, and perception
content.pronunciation: SHORT o as in not or top (never the long oh sound — that is Omega ω)

verse_connections.note: Heaven here is οὐρανοῖς — an Omicron word. Every time we pray the Lord's Prayer we begin with this letter
verse_connections.text: Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name

verse_connections.note: Heaven (οὐρανοῦ) — the destination of the overcomers. Omicron opens the most important preposition in this promise
verse_connections.text: Him that overcometh will I make a pillar in the temple of my God, and he shall go no more out: and I will write upon him the name of my God, and the name of the city of my God, which is new Jerusalem, which cometh down out of heaven

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: SHORT o as in not (never long oh)
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Ayin 𐤏 — eye, symbolising seeing and insight

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: I am the way, the truth, and the life
Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name

alphabet.title: Pi (Π π)
alphabet.subtitle: The sixteenth letter — Faith & Spirit

content.name: Pi
content.fun_fact: πίστις (pistis) means faith and appears 244 times in the NT. The just shall live by πίστις (Rom 1:17) — Paul's core thesis from Habakkuk. Also: πνεῦμα (pneuma) = Spirit and πατήρ (patēr) = Father. The Father, the Spirit, and our faith — all begin with Pi!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Π?
What does πίστις (pistis) mean?
Which three important NT words all begin with Pi (π)?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Pe (mouth 𐤐) — represents speech, expression, and the spoken word
content.pronunciation: p as in pen (clear, never aspirated with breath)

verse_connections.note: Faith is πίστις — the Pi word that Paul builds his entire letter to Rome upon. Everything flows from πίστις
verse_connections.text: For therein is the righteousness of God revealed from faith to faith: as it is written, The just shall live by faith

verse_connections.note: The most famous definition of πίστις in all of Scripture. Faith is not blind — it is the evidence, the title deed of things not yet visible
verse_connections.text: Now faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: p as in pen
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Pe 𐤐 — mouth, symbolising speech and expression

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Now faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen
God is a Spirit: and they that worship him must worship him in spirit and in truth

alphabet.title: Rho (Ρ ρ)
alphabet.subtitle: The seventeenth letter — The P that sounds like R

content.name: Rho
content.fun_fact: VISUAL TRAP: Uppercase Ρ looks exactly like English P — but it is always the R sound! The famous Chi-Rho symbol ☧ uses this letter: X is Chi (Ch) and P is Rho (R) together = ChR = Christos (Christ). Emperor Constantine saw this symbol before his famous victory!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Ρ?
WARNING: Rho (Ρ) looks like which English letter but sounds completely different?
The Chi-Rho symbol (☧) is made of Chi (Χ) and Rho (Ρ). What word do these two letters begin?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Resh (head 𐤓) — represents headship, the top, authority, and the beginning of something
content.pronunciation: r, ideally rolled or trilled like Spanish r (English r also acceptable)

verse_connections.note: Shepherd in Greek is ποιμήν but the Rho-derived word ῥῆμα (spoken word) flows from this — Christ speaks as the shepherd-king whose word carries authority
verse_connections.text: I am the good shepherd: the good shepherd giveth his life for the sheep

verse_connections.note: The Lamb as shepherd-king (ποιμανεῖ) — the Chi-Rho (Christos) who leads His flock to eternal springs
verse_connections.text: For the Lamb which is in the midst of the throne shall feed them, and shall lead them unto living fountains of waters

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: r, rolled or trilled — LOOKS like P but is R!
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Resh 𐤓 — head, symbolising headship and authority

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of God
There shall be a root of Jesse, and he that shall rise to reign over the Gentiles

alphabet.title: Sigma (Σ σ ς)
alphabet.subtitle: The eighteenth letter — The two-faced letter

content.name: Sigma
content.fun_fact: Sigma is the only Greek letter with TWO lowercase forms: σ is used at the beginning or middle of a word, and ς (final sigma) is used ONLY at the end of a word. Χριστός (Christos) ends with ς! Watch for this in your NT Greek — it helps you identify word boundaries.

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Σ?
Sigma has two lowercase forms. Which form appears at the END of a word?
The word Χριστός (Christos = Christ) ends in which letter?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Shin (teeth 𐤔) — represents sharpness, the pressing or hissing sound
content.pronunciation: s as in sun (never z)

verse_connections.note: Christ is Χριστός ending in final sigma (ς). Peter's great confession contains the Sigma letter that marks the end of the word — and the turning point of the Gospels
verse_connections.text: And Simon Peter answered and said, Thou art the Christ, the Son of the living God

verse_connections.note: Salvation (σωτηρία) begins with Sigma — the letter whose shape echoes the curve of a protecting arm around God's people
verse_connections.text: Neither is there salvation in any other: for there is none other name under heaven given among men, whereby we must be saved

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: s as in sun; σ mid-word, ς end-of-word
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Shin 𐤔 — teeth, symbolising sharpness

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Neither is there salvation in any other: for there is none other name
But God forbid that I should glory, save in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ

alphabet.title: Tau (Τ τ)
alphabet.subtitle: The nineteenth letter — The cross-shaped letter

content.name: Tau
content.fun_fact: Tau is the last letter of the Hebrew alphabet (Tav). In Ezekiel 9:4, God commanded His angel to put a mark (tav) on the foreheads of the faithful before judgment — and the ancient tav was cross-shaped! Early Christians saw this as a prophecy of the cross. The cross is literally written into the alphabet!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Τ?
The ancient Phoenician form of Tau (Tav) was shaped like a cross or X. In Ezekiel 9:4, what was this mark used for?
What is the numeric value of Tau?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Tav (mark / cross 𐤕) — in ancient form the Tav was written as an X or + shape. It was the LAST letter of the Hebrew alphabet, used as a seal or mark
content.pronunciation: t as in top (clean, not aspirated)

verse_connections.note: The mark (tav in Hebrew) was cross-shaped in ancient script. God's protective seal over His people — a foreshadowing of the blood of the cross
verse_connections.text: Go through the midst of the city, through the midst of Jerusalem, and set a mark upon the foreheads of the men that sigh and that cry for all the abominations that be done in the midst thereof

verse_connections.note: Cross is σταυρός — but Paul's boast is in what the Tau-shaped Tav always pointed to: the instrument of redemption
verse_connections.text: But God forbid that I should glory, save in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: t as in top (clean, not aspirated)
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Tav 𐤕 — ancient cross/mark, the seal of the faithful

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: To them gave he power to become the sons of God, even to them that believe on his name
For Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth

alphabet.title: Upsilon (Υ υ)
alphabet.subtitle: The twentieth letter — Son & Sonship

content.name: Upsilon
content.fun_fact: υἱός (huios) means son and is used 382 times in the NT. The central claim of the NT is υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ — Son of God. For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten υἱός (John 3:16). Upsilon carries the weight of the greatest declaration of love ever made.

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Υ?
What does υἱός (huios) mean?
How many times does υἱός (son) appear in the NT — showing how central sonship is to the gospel?

content.pictograph: From Phoenician Vav (hook / peg 𐤅) — represents connection, joining, and being fastened to something
content.pronunciation: Similar to French u or German ü — round your lips as if to say oo, then say ee. No direct English equivalent

verse_connections.note: Son is υἱόν — the Upsilon word at the heart of the entire gospel. The Father gave His Son (υἱός) so we might become sons
verse_connections.text: For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life

verse_connections.note: The Father's declaration at Jesus' baptism: υἱός μου ὁ ἀγαπητός — my beloved Son. Upsilon carries the Father's love in this moment
verse_connections.text: And lo a voice from heaven, saying, This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: ü (round lips for oo, say ee) — no English equivalent
_alphabet.pictographic: Phoenician Vav 𐤅 — hook/peg, symbolising connection and joining

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: He gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish
But whosoever drinketh of the water that I shall give him shall never thirst

alphabet.title: Phi (Φ φ)
alphabet.subtitle: The twenty-first letter — Light & Revelation

content.name: Phi
content.fun_fact: φῶς (phōs) means light — God is φῶς (1 John 1:5). The English word photograph means light-writing (φῶς + γράφω). Φωτισμός (phōtismos) means illumination — the same root is translated as enlightened in Hebrews 6:4. When God's Word enters, it brings phōs!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Φ?
What does φῶς (phōs) mean?
The word photograph comes from Greek. Photo means light (φῶς). What does graph mean (γράφω)?

content.pictograph: A uniquely Greek letter, developed from an early variant of Pi. The circle with a line through it may represent a torch or burning flame
content.pronunciation: f as in phone (a single f sound — not p-h as two letters)

verse_connections.note: Light is φῶς — the Phi word. Christ the φῶς enters the darkness of this world and the darkness cannot overcome it
verse_connections.text: And the light shineth in darkness; and the darkness comprehended it not

verse_connections.note: God is φῶς — absolute, perfect light. No shadow, no darkness. Phi carries one of the most profound statements about God's nature
verse_connections.text: This then is the message which we have heard of him, and declare unto you, that God is light, and in him is no darkness at all

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: f as in phone (single f sound)
_alphabet.pictographic: Greek invention — circle with vertical line, possibly representing a torch

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And the light shineth in darkness; and the darkness comprehended it not
My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me

alphabet.title: Chi (Χ χ)
alphabet.subtitle: The twenty-second letter — The Christ letter

content.name: Chi
content.fun_fact: Χριστός (Christos) = the Anointed One / Christ begins with Chi. The X in Xmas is NOT removing Christ from Christmas — X is Chi, the first letter of Christ's title in Greek! Early Christians used ☧ (Chi-Rho, the first two letters of Christos) as their secret symbol. This is the Christ letter.

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Χ?
The X in Xmas represents Chi (Χ), the first letter of which Greek word?
How should Chi (Χ) be pronounced?

content.pictograph: A uniquely Greek letter (though the shape influenced later alphabets). Its X shape has made it historically significant
content.pronunciation: ch as in the Scottish loch or German Bach — a guttural sound in the throat, NOT ch as in church

verse_connections.note: Christ is Χριστός — the Chi word. Every time Christ is written in Greek, the Chi letter appears first, secretly displaying the cross-shaped X
verse_connections.text: And Jacob begat Joseph the husband of Mary, of whom was born Jesus, who is called Christ

verse_connections.note: Christians (Χριστιανοί) begins with Chi. The followers of the Χριστός were named after Him — identified by the Chi-letter from the very beginning
verse_connections.text: And the disciples were called Christians first in Antioch

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: ch as in Scottish loch (guttural, NOT church)
_alphabet.pictographic: Greek invention — X shape, the cross-mark of Christ

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Of whom was born Jesus, who is called Christ
For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves

alphabet.title: Psi (Ψ ψ)
alphabet.subtitle: The twenty-third letter — Soul & Inner Life

content.name: Psi
content.fun_fact: ψυχή (psychē) means soul or inner life and appears 103 times in the NT. Our word psychology literally means study of the soul (ψυχή + λόγος). Jesus asked: what is a man profited, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own ψυχή? (Matt 16:26). Your soul has eternal value!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Ψ?
What does ψυχή (psychē) mean — and what English word comes from it?
Jesus asked what profit is it to gain the whole world but lose your ψυχή. What is ψυχή?

content.pictograph: A uniquely Greek letter. Its trident shape may represent the soul reaching upward in three aspects: mind, will, and emotion
content.pronunciation: ps blended together as in lips or maps (one sound at the START of a word — unusual in English but common in Greek)

verse_connections.note: Soul is ψυχή — the Psi word. Jesus places infinite value on the eternal soul that Psi represents
verse_connections.text: For what is a man profited, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul? or what shall a man give in exchange for his soul?

verse_connections.note: Soul (ψυχή) named alongside spirit (πνεῦμα) and body (σῶμα). The full person — Psi represents the middle dimension of our being
verse_connections.text: And the very God of peace sanctify you wholly; and I pray God your whole spirit and soul and body be preserved blameless

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: ps blended as in lips (one sound at start of word)
_alphabet.pictographic: Greek invention — trident shape, soul reaching upward

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: What shall a man give in exchange for his soul?
Speaking to yourselves in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs

alphabet.title: Omega (Ω ω)
alphabet.subtitle: The twenty-fourth letter — The End & Eternity

content.name: Omega
content.fun_fact: Omega is the LAST letter of the Greek alphabet. Jesus declares I am the Alpha AND the Omega (Rev 22:13) — the first letter and the last letter, the beginning and the end. He encompasses the entire alphabet, which means He encompasses every word, every story, every life. From A to Z is entirely contained within Christ!

practice.question: What is the name of this Greek letter: Ω?
Omega is the LAST Greek letter. Omicron is the short o. Omega is the long o. What does Omega literally mean?

options: He is first and last, the beginning and end, encompassing all
He is eternal only in the future
practice.question: Jesus declares I am the Alpha and Omega in Revelation. What does this title mean?

content.pictograph: Omega developed as an extended form of Omicron — ὦ μέγα means great O. It was added to the alphabet to distinguish the long and short o sounds
content.pronunciation: LONG oh as in tone or home — always long, always held. Contrast with Omicron (short o)

verse_connections.note: From Α (Alpha) to Ω (Omega) — the first declaration of Christ as the totality of existence. All 24 letters find their meaning in Him
verse_connections.text: I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the ending, saith the Lord, which is, and which was, and which is to come, the Almighty

verse_connections.note: The final Omega — the last letter of the last book speaks the last self-declaration of Christ. We end where we began: in Him. Every letter of this alphabet was a step toward knowing the One who IS the Word
verse_connections.text: I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end, the first and the last

_alphabet.pronunciation_guide: LONG oh as in tone — always long, contrast omicron
_alphabet.pictographic: Extended form of Omicron — ὦ μέγα, the great O

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: The hour is come, that the Son of man should be glorified
For what shall it profit a man, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul?

grammar.title: The Definite Article
grammar.subtitle: ὁ ἡ τό — Beginner

examples.note: Masculine singular nominative — θεός is masculine
examples.translation: the God

examples.note: Feminine singular nominative — ἀγάπη is feminine
examples.translation: the love

examples.note: Neuter singular nominative — εὐαγγέλιον is neuter
examples.translation: the gospel

examples.note: Masculine plural nominative — the article changes to οἱ for plural
examples.translation: the disciples

content.overview: Greek has only one article — the definite article (equivalent to the in English). There is no indefinite article (a/an) — that meaning is shown simply by the absence of the article. What makes Greek unique is that the article must agree with its noun in gender (masculine/feminine/neuter), number (singular/plural), and case (nominative, genitive, dative, accusative, vocative). This gives 24 distinct forms of the article.

practice.question: What is the masculine singular nominative form of the Greek definite article?
A Greek noun with NO article means:

options: It means Jesus is not God
It shows the Word shares God's divine nature without being identified as the entire Godhead
practice.question: John 1:1 says the Word WAS God (θεὸς ἦν ὁ λόγος). The word God (θεός) has no article here. Why is this theologically important?

key_points: Greek has NO indefinite article — a God is simply θεός (no article)
ὁ is masculine, ἡ is feminine, τό is neuter
The article agrees with its noun in gender, number, and case
The presence of the article is often theologically significant: ὁ θεός = the God (the specific, true God)
The article can turn almost any word into a noun: ὁ λόγος = the word, οἱ πιστοί = the faithful ones

verse_connections.note: ὁ λόγος (the Word, with article) vs θεὸς ἦν (was God, no article). The presence/absence of the article carries the entire Trinitarian argument here
verse_connections.text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God

verse_connections.note: ὁ πατὴρ ἡμῶν — the Father of us (with article). The article on Father makes it specific — not any father but the definite, personal Father in heaven
verse_connections.text: Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name

grammar.title: Noun Gender
grammar.subtitle: Masculine, Feminine & Neuter — Beginner

examples.note: -ος ending → typically masculine
examples.translation: the word (masculine)

examples.note: -η ending → typically feminine
examples.translation: the love (feminine)

examples.note: Irregular — πνεῦμα is neuter despite referring to the personal Holy Spirit
examples.translation: the spirit (neuter)

examples.note: -η ending → feminine
examples.translation: the soul (feminine)

content.overview: Every Greek noun has a fixed grammatical gender: masculine (ὁ), feminine (ἡ), or neuter (τό). Gender does not always match natural sex — for example, τέκνον (child) is neuter, and ψυχή (soul) is feminine. You must memorise each noun with its gender (always given by the article). Fortunately, there are patterns: most nouns ending in -ος are masculine, most ending in -η or -α are feminine, and most ending in -ον are neuter.

practice.question: What article tells you a noun is feminine?
The word εὐαγγέλιον (gospel) ends in -ον. What gender is it likely to be?

options: The Spirit is an impersonal force
Grammatical gender is not the same as natural sex — the Spirit's personhood is shown by other features like pronouns
The Spirit is less than a person
practice.question: τὸ πνεῦμα (the Spirit) is grammatically neuter, yet the Holy Spirit is a Person. What does this tell us about grammatical gender?

key_points: Every noun has a fixed gender — must be memorised with the word
The article tells you the gender: ὁ = masculine, ἡ = feminine, τό = neuter
Gender patterns: -ος usually masculine, -η/-α usually feminine, -ον usually neuter
Natural sex and grammatical gender sometimes differ (τέκνον = child is neuter)
Adjectives and articles must AGREE in gender with their noun

verse_connections.note: τὸ πνεῦμα ὁ θεός — Spirit (neuter τό) is equated with God (masculine ὁ). Grammatical gender does not restrict personal divine attributes
verse_connections.text: God is a Spirit: and they that worship him must worship him in spirit and in truth

verse_connections.note: The pronoun ἐκεῖνος (that one, masculine) is used for the Spirit (πνεῦμα, neuter) — Jesus deliberately uses a masculine pronoun to assert the Spirit's personhood over grammatical gender
verse_connections.text: But the Comforter, which is the Holy Ghost, whom the Father will send in my name

grammar.title: The Five Noun Cases
grammar.subtitle: Function determines ending — Beginner

tables.title: The Five Cases and Their Functions

examples.note: θεός = nominative (subject); κόσμον = accusative (object). The endings tell you who loves and who is loved
examples.translation: God loves the world

examples.note: τοῦ θεοῦ = genitive (of God) — the genitive shows the source/possessor
examples.translation: the grace of God

examples.note: υἱόν = accusative (object of the preposition εἰς); θεοῦ = genitive (of God)
examples.greek: πιστεύω εἰς τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ θεοῦ
examples.translation: I believe into the Son of God

content.overview: Greek nouns change their endings (called inflections or declensions) to show their grammatical function in a sentence. This system of endings is called cases. Greek has 5 cases: Nominative (subject), Genitive (possession/of), Dative (indirect object/to/for/by), Accusative (direct object), and Vocative (direct address). Because the ending shows function, Greek word order is very flexible — a word can be moved for emphasis without changing meaning.

practice.question: Which case marks the subject of a verb in Greek?
In John 3:16 God loved the world, the word world (κόσμον) is in what case — because it receives the action of loving?
ἡ χάρις τοῦ θεοῦ means the grace of God. The word God (θεοῦ) is in which case, showing possession/source?

key_points: Nominative: the subject doing the action — ὁ θεός ἀγαπᾷ (God loves)
Genitive: possession or association — τοῦ θεοῦ (of God)
Dative: indirect object, means, or location — τῷ θεῷ (to/for God)
Accusative: the direct object receiving the action — τὸν θεόν (God as object)
Vocative: direct address — ὦ θεέ! (O God!)
Because endings show function, Greek word order is flexible — emphasis, not grammar, drives order

verse_connections.note: ὁ θεός (nom) — subject; τὸν κόσμον (acc) — object; τὸν υἱόν (acc) — object of gave. Cases unlock the grammar of the most famous verse in the Bible
verse_connections.text: For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son

verse_connections.note: τοῖς ἀγαπῶσιν τὸν θεόν — to them that love (dative, indirect object) God (accusative, direct object). Cases show who receives the benefit and who receives the love
verse_connections.text: And we know that all things work together for good to them that love God

grammar.title: First Declension Nouns
grammar.subtitle: Mostly feminine — ἀγάπη pattern — Beginner

examples.note: The ἀγάπη pattern — pure first declension feminine
examples.translation: Nom: ἀγάπη · Gen: ἀγάπης · Dat: ἀγάπῃ · Acc: ἀγάπην

examples.note: Same pattern as ἀγάπη — note how the accent moves
examples.translation: Nom: ψυχή · Gen: ψυχῆς · Dat: ψυχῇ · Acc: ψυχήν

examples.note: Masculine first declension — notice the genitive takes -οῦ not -ης
examples.translation: Nom: μαθητής · Gen: μαθητοῦ · Dat: μαθητῇ · Acc: μαθητήν

content.overview: Greek nouns are grouped into three declension patterns based on their endings. First declension nouns mostly end in -η or -α in the nominative singular and are predominantly feminine. They follow a regular, predictable pattern. Key first declension words include ἀγάπη (love), ψυχή (soul), εἰρήνη (peace), and ἐκκλησία (church) — some of the most theologically important NT words.

practice.question: What case ending marks the genitive singular of a first declension feminine noun like ἀγάπη?
Most first declension nouns are which gender?
Which of these is a masculine first declension noun?

key_points: First declension: mostly feminine, ending in -η or -α (nominative singular)
The genitive singular always ends in -ης or -ας
The dative singular ends in -ῃ or -ᾳ
The accusative singular ends in -ην or -αν
Plural endings are the same for all first declension nouns: -αι, -ῶν, -αις, -ᾱς
A few first declension nouns are masculine (ending -ης or -ας): μαθητής, προφήτης

verse_connections.note: ἀγάπη — nominative singular first declension. The greatest virtue in Paul's famous trilogy takes the most fundamental first declension form
verse_connections.text: And now abideth faith, hope, charity, these three; but the greatest of these is charity

verse_connections.note: εἰρήνην — accusative singular first declension (object of give). Christ's parting gift is in the accusative — something He actively gives to us
verse_connections.text: Peace I leave with you, my peace I give unto you

grammar.title: Second Declension Nouns
grammar.subtitle: Mostly masculine — λόγος & ἔργον patterns — Beginner

examples.note: The standard masculine second declension pattern — memorise this!
examples.translation: Nom: λόγος · Gen: λόγου · Dat: λόγῳ · Acc: λόγον

examples.note: Same exact pattern as λόγος — once you know one, you know them all
examples.translation: Nom: κόσμος · Gen: κόσμου · Dat: κόσμῳ · Acc: κόσμον

examples.note: Neuter: nominative = accusative (both -ον). Same dative and genitive as masculine
examples.translation: Nom: ἔργον · Gen: ἔργου · Dat: ἔργῳ · Acc: ἔργον

content.overview: Second declension nouns end in -ος (masculine) or -ον (neuter) in the nominative singular. This is the largest and most regular Greek declension, containing many key theological words: λόγος (Word), κύριος (Lord), νόμος (law), κόσμος (world), σταυρός (cross), and οὐρανός (heaven). The masculine and neuter forms are identical except in nominative, accusative, and vocative.

practice.question: What ending marks the genitive singular of a second declension noun (like λόγος)?
In neuter second declension nouns, which two cases always have identical forms?
Which of these is a second declension masculine noun?

key_points: Masculine second declension: -ος nominative singular (ὁ λόγος)
Neuter second declension: -ον nominative singular (τὸ ἔργον)
Neuter rule: nominative and accusative are ALWAYS identical in neuter nouns
The genitive singular ends in -ου for both masculine and neuter
Second declension contains the most common Greek nouns
Key vocab: λόγος, κύριος, νόμος, κόσμος, δοῦλος, ἀδελφός, ἄνθρωπος

verse_connections.note: ὁ λόγος — nominative (subject). τοῦ θεοῦ would be genitive. Every case of this second declension noun carries theological weight in John's prologue
verse_connections.text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God

verse_connections.note: τὸν κόσμον — accusative singular (object of loved). The world (κόσμος, second declension masculine) is the object of God's love. The -ν ending marks it as accusative
verse_connections.text: For God so loved the world

grammar.title: Third Declension Nouns
grammar.subtitle: All three genders — σάρξ & πνεῦμα patterns — Beginner

examples.note: Genitive -ός reveals the true stem: σαρκ-. The nominative σάρξ looks different!
examples.translation: Nom: σάρξ · Gen: σαρκός · Dat: σαρκί · Acc: σάρκα

examples.note: The -ις/-εως pattern is common for abstract nouns like ἐκκλησία, κρίσις, σωτηρία
examples.translation: Nom: πίστις · Gen: πίστεως · Dat: πίστει · Acc: πίστιν

examples.note: Neuter -μα nouns are very common; genitive always -ματος
examples.translation: Nom: πνεῦμα · Gen: πνεύματος · Dat: πνεύματι · Acc: πνεῦμα

content.overview: The third declension is the most diverse of the three, containing nouns of all three genders. Nominative singulars vary widely, making these harder to recognise. The key identifier is the genitive singular, which always ends in -ος (not -ου like second declension). Third declension includes some crucial NT words: σάρξ (flesh), πίστις (faith), πνεῦμα (spirit), σωτήρ (Saviour), and πατήρ (Father).

practice.question: What ending always marks the genitive singular of a third declension noun?

options: Look at the genitive: σαρκός → stem is σαρκ-
practice.question: John 1:14 says the Word became flesh (σάρξ). How would you find the stem of this third declension word?

options: Genitive and dative are always the same
Nominative and accusative are always identical
practice.question: Neuter third declension nouns follow which rule?

key_points: Third declension has nouns of all three genders
Genitive singular always ends in -ος (e.g. σαρκός, πίστεως, πνεύματος)
The dative plural always ends in -σι(ν)
Nominative singular forms vary greatly — no single pattern
Drop the genitive -ος to find the stem: σάρξ → σαρκ-; πίστις → πίστε-
Neuter third declension follows the neuter rule: nominative = accusative

verse_connections.note: σάρξ — third declension nominative. The eternal λόγος (second declension) became σάρξ (third declension) — two different declension patterns united in the Incarnation
verse_connections.text: And the Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us

verse_connections.note: ἡ σάρξ κατὰ τοῦ πνεύματος — flesh (third declension) against the Spirit (third declension genitive πνεύματος). The great inner conflict of the Christian life described in two third declension words
verse_connections.text: For the flesh lusteth against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh

grammar.title: Greek Adjectives
grammar.subtitle: Agreement in gender, number & case — Beginner

examples.note: Attributive position — adjective between article and noun, all masculine nominative
examples.translation: the good shepherd

examples.note: Predicate position — adjective outside the article, making a statement about the noun
examples.translation: God is good

examples.note: αἰώνιον agrees with ζωήν — both accusative feminine singular
examples.translation: eternal life

content.overview: Greek adjectives must agree with their noun in three features: gender, number, and case. This is called concord. An adjective has 24 possible forms (3 genders × 2 numbers × 4 cases, roughly). Most adjectives follow first-and-second declension patterns (masculine/neuter = second declension; feminine = first declension). Two positions: attributive (inside the article-noun bracket) and predicate (outside, with no article or separated by a form of to be).

practice.question: An adjective must agree with its noun in which three features?

options: The adjective comes after the noun
The adjective is inside the article-noun bracket
practice.question: In the phrase ὁ ἀγαθὸς ποιμήν (the good shepherd), the adjective ἀγαθός is in attributive position. How do you recognise attributive position?

options: The adjective αἰώνιον agrees with ζωήν — both are feminine accusative singular
They are in the nominative case
practice.question: ζωὴν αἰώνιον (eternal life) — both words end in -ον/-ην. What does this agreement tell you?

key_points: Adjectives agree with their noun in gender, number, AND case — all three must match
Attributive position: ὁ ἀγαθὸς ἄνθρωπος = the good man (article-adj-noun)
Predicate position: ὁ ἄνθρωπος ἀγαθός ἐστιν = the man IS good (adj outside article)
Most NT adjectives follow 2-1-2 declension: masc/neut = 2nd declension, fem = 1st
Common adjectives: ἀγαθός (good), πιστός (faithful), ἅγιος (holy), αἰώνιος (eternal), πᾶς (all)
The adjective πᾶς (all, every) is a 3rd-1st-3rd declension — irregular but essential

verse_connections.note: ὁ ποιμὴν ὁ καλός — the shepherd the good (double article attributive). Jesus uses the adjective καλός (beautiful-good, noble) rather than ἀγαθός — emphasising visible, excellent goodness
verse_connections.text: I am the good shepherd: the good shepherd giveth his life for the sheep

verse_connections.note: ζωὴν αἰώνιον — eternal life (accusative feminine). The two-word phrase that defines the promise of the gospel — adjective and noun in perfect grammatical agreement
verse_connections.text: that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life

grammar.title: Personal Pronouns
grammar.subtitle: ἐγώ σύ αὐτός — Beginner

examples.note: ἐγώ is emphatic — Jesus is asserting His identity, not just stating a fact. The I AM echoes Exodus 3:14
examples.greek: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ποιμὴν ὁ καλός
examples.translation: I AM the good shepherd

examples.note: σύ is emphatic — Peter's great confession singles out Jesus as THE Christ
examples.translation: You are the Christ

examples.note: αὐτός emphatic (he himself); αὐτοῦ = his (genitive possession)
examples.translation: He himself will save his people

content.overview: Greek personal pronouns decline for case (like nouns) but also encode person (1st, 2nd, 3rd) and number. First person: ἐγώ (I), ἡμεῖς (we). Second person: σύ (you singular), ὑμεῖς (you plural). Third person: αὐτός/αὐτή/αὐτό (he/she/it), αὐτοί/αὐταί/αὐτά (they). A key feature: Greek VERBS already encode person and number in their endings, so subject pronouns are often omitted — when they appear, they carry emphasis.

practice.answer: It is emphatic — I (and no one else). It echoes God's self-declaration in Exodus 3:14 (I AM THAT I AM)
practice.question: In the seven I AM statements of Jesus (John 6:35, 8:12, 10:9, etc.), what is the emphatic significance of the explicit ἐγώ?

options: It is emphatic — stressing I as opposed to someone else
practice.question: Greek verbs already encode person in their endings. When the subject pronoun ἐγώ is used explicitly anyway, what does it indicate?
αὐτοῦ is the genitive of αὐτός. What does it mean in Matthew 1:21 (he shall save his people)?

key_points: Greek verbs carry person/number in their endings — subject pronouns are often omitted
When ἐγώ or σύ appears explicitly, it is emphatic: I (not someone else)
The I AM (ἐγώ εἰμι) statements of Jesus are emphatic — the explicit ἐγώ is emphatic deity language
αὐτός = he/him; αὐτή = she/her; αὐτό = it; αὐτοί = they
αὐτός can also mean same or himself as an intensive pronoun
The genitive of the personal pronoun shows possession: αὐτοῦ = of him/his

verse_connections.note: ἐγώ εἰμι — the most explosive I AM claim. The explicit ἐγώ before εἰμι is emphatic and deliberate. The audience understood the divine Name claim — they picked up stones to stone Him
verse_connections.text: Jesus said unto them, Verily, verily, I say unto you, Before Abraham was, I am

verse_connections.note: ζῶ δὲ οὐκέτι ἐγώ — I no longer live. The explicit ἐγώ is emphatic — Paul means specifically the old self, which has died. Then ζῇ δὲ ἐν ἐμοὶ Χριστός — Christ lives in me (ἐμοί, dative)
verse_connections.text: I am crucified with Christ: nevertheless I live; yet not I, but Christ liveth in me

grammar.title: Greek Prepositions
grammar.subtitle: ἐν εἰς ἐκ ἀπό διά — Beginner

examples.note: ἐν + dative — the most common phrase in Paul's letters (appears 90+ times). Location/sphere: our entire spiritual life exists in the sphere of Christ
examples.translation: in Christ

examples.note: εἰς + accusative — direction/purpose. Baptism is a movement INTO identity with the Name of the Trinity
examples.translation: baptize into the name

examples.note: ἐκ + genitive — movement out from. Jesus does not pray to take us out of the world but to protect us in it
examples.translation: out of the world

examples.note: διά + genitive — means/agent. Grace reaches us THROUGH faith as its channel
examples.translation: through faith

content.overview: Greek prepositions show the relationship between nouns/pronouns and the rest of a sentence (location, direction, means, etc.). Crucially, Greek prepositions govern specific cases — they tell you which case to use for the noun that follows. Some prepositions take only one case; others take two or three depending on meaning. Understanding which case a preposition governs is essential for NT interpretation.

practice.question: What case does ἐν (in) always take?

options: Location/sphere — our entire life is situated within Christ
practice.question: Paul writes ἐν Χριστῷ (in Christ) over 90 times. What does the preposition ἐν with dative express here?

options: Baptism is a past event only
Baptism is a movement INTO identification with the Name — a relational union
Baptism is just a symbol with no directional meaning
Baptism requires a formula not a relationship
practice.question: εἰς τὸ ὄνομα (into the name) — Matthew 28:19 on baptism. εἰς takes the accusative. What does the directional sense of εἰς add to baptism?

key_points: Prepositions govern specific cases — the case affects the meaning
ἐν (in) always takes the dative case
εἰς (into, for the purpose of) always takes the accusative case
ἐκ/ἐξ (out of, from) always takes the genitive case
ἀπό (away from) always takes the genitive
διά (through/because of) takes genitive (agent/means) or accusative (reason)
πρός (toward, with) takes accusative; κατά (against/according to) takes genitive or accusative

verse_connections.note: ἐν Χριστῷ — in Christ (dative). Paul's great declaration of positional blessings. Every spiritual blessing we have exists in the sphere of our union with Christ
verse_connections.text: Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who hath blessed us with all spiritual blessings in heavenly places in Christ

verse_connections.note: ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ — in Christ Jesus (dative). The most liberating no condemnation in Scripture is grounded entirely in the preposition ἐν and the dative — our position in Christ
verse_connections.text: There is therefore now no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus

grammar.title: Present Active Indicative
grammar.subtitle: λύω — I loose · Ongoing action — Intermediate

tables.title: How to Parse a Greek Verb

examples.note: 1st singular present active — πιστεύ- (stem) + -ω (1st sg ending). Ongoing personal faith
examples.greek: πιστεύω εἰς τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ θεοῦ
examples.translation: I believe into the Son of God

examples.note: 2nd singular present active — γινώσκ- + -εις. Jesus challenges Nicodemus
examples.translation: Do you not know?

examples.note: 3rd singular present active contracted — ἀγαπά- + -ει → ἀγαπᾷ (contracted). Timeless love
examples.translation: The Father loves the Son

content.overview: Greek verbs are the heart of the language. Every Greek verb encodes six pieces of information: person (1st/2nd/3rd), number (singular/plural), tense (time + kind of action), voice (active/middle/passive), mood (indicative/subjunctive/imperative), and sometimes aspect. The Present Active Indicative is the foundation: it describes ongoing, continuous, or habitual action from the perspective of the speaker as real (indicative mood). Memorising the λύω paradigm gives you the pattern for hundreds of NT verbs.

practice.question: What are the six personal endings of the Present Active Indicative in order?
The verb πιστεύουσιν appears in John 12:37 (yet they believed not). What person and number is this?
The present tense in Greek primarily expresses what kind of action?

key_points: Greek verbs encode person and number in the ending — subject pronouns often omitted
Present tense: ongoing or habitual action (I am loosing / I loose / I keep loosing)
Active voice: the subject performs the action
Indicative mood: stating a fact (vs. subjunctive = possibility)
The personal endings: -ω, -εις, -ει, -ομεν, -ετε, -ουσι(ν)
The connecting vowel -ο/-ε- links the stem to the personal ending

verse_connections.note: πιστεύων — present active participle (believing, ongoing). The ongoing nature of saving faith is embedded in the present tense. Not a one-time act but a continuous trusting
verse_connections.text: For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life

verse_connections.note: ὁ μὴ ἀγαπῶν οὐκ ἔγνω τὸν θεόν — the one not loving (present) has not known God. Present tense loving is John's litmus test of genuine knowledge of God
verse_connections.text: He that loveth not knoweth not God; for God is love

grammar.title: Imperfect & Future Tenses
grammar.subtitle: Past ongoing · Future certain — Intermediate

examples.note: Imperfect 3rd singular — ἐδίδασκεν captures Jesus teaching as ongoing, not just one discrete act. The Sermon on the Mount was a sustained ministry
examples.translation: He was teaching them (ongoing)

examples.note: Future active — σώσ- (future stem) + -ει (3rd sg). The angel's announcement is future certain: HE WILL save
examples.translation: He will save his people

examples.note: Imperfect of εἰμί + present participle — a periphrastic construction emphasising the continuous, habitual nature of Jesus' teaching ministry
examples.translation: He was (continually) teaching

content.overview: The Imperfect tense describes ongoing or repeated action in the PAST — I was loosing, I kept loosing. It is formed by adding an augment (ε-) to the front of the verb, then using secondary endings. The Future tense describes action yet to happen — I will loose. It uses a sigma (σ) inserted between stem and ending. Both tenses are crucial for NT narrative and prophecy.

practice.answer: An augment — ε- is prefixed to the verb stem (e.g. λύω → ἔλυον)
practice.question: How is the Imperfect tense formed? What is added to the front of the verb?

options: It means He taught just once
It emphasises the ongoing, continuous nature of His teaching ministry
It indicates teaching in the future
practice.question: Matthew 5:2 says He was teaching (ἐδίδασκεν — imperfect). Why is the imperfect significant here?
The future tense in Greek inserts which letter between the stem and the ending?

key_points: Imperfect: past ongoing action. Formed with augment (ε-) + stem + secondary endings
Secondary endings: -ον, -ες, -ε(ν), -ομεν, -ετε, -ον
Augment: ε- prefixed to verb beginning; if verb begins with a vowel, the vowel lengthens
Future: adds -σ- between stem and present endings (λύ- + σ- + ω = λύσω)
The Imperfect often captures vivid narrative action: they were going, he kept teaching
The Future Indicative states something as certain/expected: I WILL save

verse_connections.note: ἐδίδασκεν — imperfect (he was teaching, ongoing). The Sermon on the Mount is introduced with an imperfect — Jesus sat and taught continuously, not in one quick statement
verse_connections.text: And he opened his mouth, and taught them, saying

verse_connections.note: σώσει — future indicative (he WILL save). The angel's announcement is future certain. The name Jesus contains the future promise — YHWH will save. The future tense carries the weight of the entire Incarnation
verse_connections.text: She shall bring forth a son, and thou shalt call his name JESUS: for he shall save his people from their sins

grammar.title: The Aorist Tense
grammar.subtitle: The unique Greek past — Point action — Intermediate

examples.note: ἠγάπησεν — 1st Aorist active. God's love for the world is presented as a complete, decisive act. The cross is its focal point — the moment when the love was consummated
examples.translation: God loved the world (as a whole event)

examples.note: Second aorist — ἐγένετο (became). The incarnation is a decisive event — a point in history when the eternal Word BECAME flesh
examples.translation: he took flesh (became incarnate)

examples.note: ἀπέθανεν — aorist of ἀποθνῄσκω. The death of Christ is a historical point-event. Paul's gospel rests on an aorist fact
examples.translation: Christ died for us

content.overview: The Aorist is perhaps the most distinctive feature of Greek grammar. While English simply says I loved, Greek can choose between the Imperfect (I was loving, ongoing) and the Aorist (I loved, as a simple event). The Aorist views action as a whole, complete event — a point or snapshot, rather than a line. It is the most common narrative past tense in the NT. It does NOT necessarily mean instantaneous action — it is about how the author views the action, not its actual duration.

practice.answer: Aorist views action as a whole complete event (point); Imperfect views action as ongoing/continuous (line)
practice.question: What is the key difference between the Aorist and Imperfect past tenses in Greek?

options: God's love was brief and is now over
God's love for the world is a complete, decisive event — consummated at the cross
God loved in the past but not now
God's love is only ongoing, never completed
practice.question: John 3:16 uses the aorist ἠγάπησεν (loved). What does this aorist communicate about God's love?

options: Because death is an ongoing action
Because Christ's death was a decisive historical event viewed as a complete whole
Because aorist is used for all past events
Because Paul was uncertain about the death
practice.question: Romans 5:8 uses the aorist ἀπέθανεν (died). Why is the aorist appropriate here?

key_points: Aorist = past action viewed as a whole event (point), not duration
Most common past tense in NT narrative
First Aorist (weak): adds -σ- and uses endings: -α, -ας, -ε, -αμεν, -ατε, -αν
Second Aorist (strong): irregular stem change, uses imperfect endings: ε-stem-ον
Aorist imperative is common for urgent, decisive commands
The Aorist does NOT inherently mean the action was brief — it means the author views it as a whole unit

verse_connections.note: ἠγάπησεν...ἔδωκεν — both aorists. God LOVED (decisive event) and GAVE (decisive event). The gospel hinges on two aorist facts — two complete historical acts of divine grace
verse_connections.text: For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son

verse_connections.note: ἀπέθανεν — aorist (Christ died, as a historical fact). Paul's entire argument for justification rests on this aorist event. The past tense of the cross is the foundation of present peace with God
verse_connections.text: But God commendeth his love toward us, in that, while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us

grammar.title: Perfect & Pluperfect Tenses
grammar.subtitle: Past action with present results — Intermediate

rows: I have given (and it remains given)
Christ's gift of glory is permanently bestowed
tables.title: Key NT Perfect Verbs

examples.note: Perfect passive — Christ's atoning work is not only complete in the past but its effects are permanently in force. The perfect tense makes this claim theologically decisive
examples.translation: It is finished / It has been finished and remains so

examples.note: Perfect passive of γράφω. Jesus quotes Scripture in the perfect: it has been written and stands written. Scripture's past composition has permanent present authority
examples.translation: It is written / It stands written

examples.note: Perfect active — Paul's past faith commitment has ongoing present reality. I have believed = and I still believe
examples.translation: I have believed and continue to believe

content.overview: The Perfect tense is unique to Greek and carries enormous theological weight. It describes a past action whose results CONTINUE INTO THE PRESENT. It is not just I loosed (aorist) but I have loosed and it is still loose. The perfect communicates completed action with ongoing, present-state consequences. The Pluperfect adds one layer: the results continued up to a past point. In NT Greek, the perfect often carries the strongest possible assertion of continuing reality.

practice.answer: The Perfect describes a past action whose results continue into the present — completed action with ongoing consequences
practice.question: What makes the Greek Perfect tense unique compared to the Aorist?

options: The work is over and done with, no longer relevant
The work was completed in the past AND its saving results are permanently in force now
Jesus was uncertain if the work was complete
practice.question: Jesus says τετέλεσται (John 19:30) — It is finished (perfect tense). What does the perfect communicate?

options: Scripture was written long ago and is now outdated
Scripture was composed in the past and its authority permanently stands in the present
practice.question: γέγραπται means it stands written (perfect passive of γράφω). When Jesus quotes this in Matthew 4, what does the perfect tense assert about Scripture?

key_points: Perfect: past action + present, continuing results. I have believed and still believe
Formed by: reduplication (first consonant + ε) + stem + -κ- + perfect endings
Perfect endings: -α, -ας, -ε, -αμεν, -ατε, -ασι(ν)
John 19:30 tetelestai (It is finished) — perfect tense: it is accomplished and the results are permanent
Hebrews 1:4 uses perfect to show Christ's ongoing exalted status after His work
The perfect passive is especially important: it has been written = γέγραπται (Scripture's permanent authority)

verse_connections.note: τετέλεσται — the perfect tense of the cross. Not merely finished as in over, but completed in a way that its redemptive results are permanently in force. The believer stands under an eternally effective atonement
verse_connections.text: When Jesus therefore had received the vinegar, he said, It is finished: and he bowed his head, and gave up the ghost

verse_connections.note: γέγραπται — it stands written. Three times Jesus rebukes Satan's temptations with this perfect passive. Scripture's past composition permanently settles every present challenge. The perfect tense IS the doctrine of Scripture's authority
verse_connections.text: But he answered and said, It is written, Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of God

grammar.title: Middle & Passive Voice
grammar.subtitle: Subject involved or acted upon — Intermediate

examples.note: Aorist passive — Jesus is the subject receiving baptism. ὑπό + genitive = the agent (by John)
examples.translation: He was baptized by John

examples.note: παρακληθήσονται — future passive. Who will comfort them? God. The divine passive leaves the agent unstated because God is the implied actor
examples.greek: μακάριοι οἱ πενθοῦντες, ὅτι αὐτοὶ παρακληθήσονται
examples.translation: Blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted

examples.note: Present passive — Christ is raised (by God the Father, implied agent). The resurrection is passive: the Father raises the Son
examples.translation: he is raised / he rises

content.overview: Greek verbs have three voices: Active (subject acts), Middle (subject acts with special interest or involvement in the action), and Passive (subject is acted upon). In the present and imperfect, middle and passive share the same forms (called middle/passive). The Passive is theologically crucial in NT Greek — the divine passive (passivum divinum) is a common idiom where God is implied as the unstated agent of the passive verb.

practice.answer: A passive verb where God is the implied but unstated agent — e.g. they shall be comforted (by God)
practice.question: What is the divine passive (passivum divinum) in NT Greek?

options: God — the divine passive implies God as actor
practice.question: Matthew 5:4 — they shall be comforted (παρακληθήσονται, future passive). Who is the unstated agent doing the comforting?

options: They are middle or passive in form but active in meaning
They only appear in the aorist
practice.question: Many important NT verbs are deponent — what does this mean?

key_points: Active: subject performs action — ἀγαπᾷ (He loves)
Middle: subject acts with reflexive involvement or personal interest in the result
Passive: subject receives action — ἀγαπᾶται (He is loved, by someone else)
Divine passive (passivum divinum): God implied as agent — they will be comforted = God will comfort them
Middle/Passive share endings in present: -ομαι, -ῃ/-ει, -εται, -όμεθα, -εσθε, -ονται
Many key NT verbs are deponent — middle or passive in form but active in meaning

verse_connections.note: παρακληθήσονται — future passive (divine passive). God will comfort the mourning. The Beatitudes are saturated with divine passives — God is always the unstated actor behind the promised blessings
verse_connections.text: Blessed are they that mourn: for they shall be comforted

verse_connections.note: ἐγήγερται — perfect passive (he has been raised and remains raised). The resurrection is both passive (God raised Him) and perfect (the results are permanently in force). The empty tomb is a perfect tense fact
verse_connections.text: And that he was buried, and that he rose again the third day according to the scriptures

grammar.title: Infinitives & Participles
grammar.subtitle: Verbal nouns and verbal adjectives — Intermediate

examples.note: Present active substantival participle — ὁ πιστεύων functions as a noun: the one who keeps on believing
examples.translation: the one believing into him / whoever believes in him

examples.note: Aorist passive participle (going, having gone) + aorist imperative (make disciples). The going is prior to and assumed; the command is the making of disciples
examples.translation: Having gone therefore, make disciples

examples.note: Infinitive as subject of δεῖ (it is necessary). The aorist passive infinitive expresses the resurrection
examples.translation: It is necessary for him to be raised

content.overview: Greek has two important verbals — word forms that are partly verb, partly something else. The Infinitive is a verbal NOUN: to loose (λύειν). Like a noun it can be a subject or object, but like a verb it has tense and voice. The Participle is a verbal ADJECTIVE: loosing (λύων). Like an adjective it modifies nouns and must agree in gender/number/case, but like a verb it has tense and voice and can take objects. Both are extremely common in the NT.

practice.answer: Participle = verbal adjective (agrees with a noun in gender/number/case); Infinitive = verbal noun (indeclinable)
practice.question: What is the key difference between a Greek Participle and an Infinitive?

options: Ongoing, continuous believing — it is not a once-only act
practice.question: John 3:16 uses ὁ πιστεύων (present participle). What does the present tense of the participle indicate?

options: Going is secondary — assumed prior action; making disciples is the main imperative
Going must precede making disciples in time only
practice.question: Matthew 28:19 — πορευθέντες (aorist participle) + μαθητεύσατε (aorist imperative). What is the relationship between going and making disciples?

key_points: Infinitive = verbal noun: can be subject, object, or complement of a sentence
Present infinitive: λύειν (to be loosing); Aorist infinitive: λῦσαι (to loose as a whole act)
The article + infinitive is very common: τὸ πιστεύειν = the believing, belief
Participle = verbal adjective: must agree with noun in gender, number, case
Present participle: ongoing action; Aorist participle: prior completed action
Substantival participle: ὁ πιστεύων = the one believing (noun use of participle)
The most famous participle clause: Great Commission — πορευθέντες...μαθητεύσατε (going...make disciples)

verse_connections.note: ὁ πιστεύων — the believing one (present participle as noun). John's Gospel consistently uses this present participle for saving faith — it is habitual, ongoing trust, not a momentary decision
verse_connections.text: that whosoever believeth in him should not perish

verse_connections.note: πορευθέντες (aorist participle: having gone/going) + μαθητεύσατε (aorist imperative: make disciples). The going is assumed; the Great Commission's singular imperative is to make disciples. The participle clarifies the method of the mission
verse_connections.text: Go ye therefore, and teach all nations

grammar.title: Key Conjunctions
grammar.subtitle: καί δέ ἀλλά γάρ ὅτι ἵνα — Intermediate

content.symbol: καί · δέ · ἀλλά · γάρ · ὅτι · ἵνα

examples.note: ἀλλά = strong contrast. Jesus sets up a sharp antithesis: NOT abolish — strong stop — BUT fulfil
examples.translation: I did not come to abolish but to fulfil

examples.note: γάρ explains the previous statement. Often the γάρ sentence is the key theological reason that unlocks the paragraph
examples.greek: ἠγάπησεν γὰρ ὁ θεὸς τὸν κόσμον
examples.translation: For God so loved the world

examples.note: ἵνα + subjunctive = purpose clause. ὅτι = that (content). John's purpose statement uses both conjunctions in one sentence
examples.greek: ἵνα πιστεύητε ὅτι Ἰησοῦς ἐστιν ὁ Χριστός
examples.translation: in order that you might believe that Jesus is the Christ

content.overview: Conjunctions are the joints of Greek sentences — they connect clauses and reveal logical relationships. Greek is rich in conjunctions, each carrying a precise nuance. Recognising conjunctions and their functions is crucial for following the argument of NT letters and the flow of Gospel narratives. Six conjunctions are absolutely essential for NT reading.

practice.answer: δέ is a mild contrast or continuation (and/but/now); ἀλλά is a strong adversative contrast (but — true opposition)
practice.question: What is the difference between δέ and ἀλλά?

options: It appears at the end of the sentence
