It never appears first in its clause — it is pushed to second position
practice.question: γάρ (for/because) is a postpositive conjunction. What does postpositive mean?
ἵνα always introduces a purpose or result clause. What mood of verb does it always take?

key_points: καί = and, also, even (the most common word in the NT — appears 9,164 times!)
δέ = but, and, now (mild contrast or continuation — sentence-second position)
ἀλλά = but (strong contrast — true adversative)
γάρ = for, because (gives the reason for what was just said — sentence-second)
ὅτι = that, because (introduces content clauses or causal clauses)
ἵνα = in order that, so that (introduces purpose or result clauses — takes subjunctive)
Postpositive conjunctions (δέ, γάρ, οὖν) never appear as first word of their clause

verse_connections.note: οὐκ...ἀλλά — not...but (strong contrast). The ἀλλά marks the pivot of the entire statement. Everything after the ἀλλά is what Jesus actually came to do — the grammar directs the theology
verse_connections.text: Think not that I am come to destroy the law, or the prophets: I am not come to destroy, but to fulfil

verse_connections.note: γάρ opens verse 16 (the for of John 3:16). This for connects back to verse 15 and the serpent/bronze snake typology. The γάρ tells us verse 16 is the reason for the preceding promise — the logic of grace
verse_connections.text: For God so loved the world...

grammar.title: Demonstrative Pronouns
grammar.subtitle: οὗτος ἐκεῖνος — This one, that one — Intermediate

examples.note: αὕτη (feminine nom sg of οὗτος) refers back to eternal life and forwards into the definition. The demonstrative makes the definition emphatic
examples.greek: αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ ζωὴ ἡ αἰώνιος
examples.translation: This is eternal life

examples.note: ἐκεῖνος used as a pronoun for the Holy Spirit (πνεῦμα, neuter) — but Jesus uses masculine ἐκεῖνος, asserting the Spirit's personhood
examples.translation: He (that one / that Counselor) will glorify me

examples.note: οὗτός (with accent shift) points directly to Jesus at His baptism. The divine voice singles Him out with the near demonstrative
examples.greek: οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ υἱός μου ὁ ἀγαπητός
examples.translation: This is my beloved Son

content.overview: Demonstrative pronouns point out specific persons or things: this/these (near) and that/those (far). Greek has two main demonstratives: οὗτος (this, near — this one here) and ἐκεῖνος (that, far — that one there). Both decline for gender, number, and case like adjectives. Their position relative to the article is distinctive: demonstratives always appear OUTSIDE the article-noun bracket (predicate position), even when used attributively.

practice.question: What is the neuter nominative singular of οὗτος (this)?

options: Jesus deliberately uses masculine to assert the Spirit's personhood over grammatical convention
practice.question: In John 16:14, Jesus uses ἐκεῖνος (masculine) for the Spirit (πνεῦμα, neuter). What is the theological significance?

options: ἡ οὗτος ἀγάπη (article first then demonstrative inside)
οὗτος ἡ ἀγάπη or ἡ ἀγάπη αὕτη (demonstrative outside article-noun bracket)
practice.question: Demonstratives always appear in predicate position relative to the article. Which phrase is correct?

key_points: οὗτος = this / these (near demonstrative — closer to speaker)
ἐκεῖνος = that / those (far demonstrative — further from speaker)
Both decline for gender, number, case — must agree with their noun
Position: always outside the article-noun bracket: οὗτος ὁ ἄνθρωπος or ὁ ἄνθρωπος οὗτος
Used substantivally (as noun): οὗτος = this one/he; ἐκεῖνος = that one/he
οὗτος can refer back to the nearest antecedent; ἐκεῖνος can refer to a more remote one

verse_connections.note: οὗτός ἐστιν ὁ υἱός μου — This is my Son. The Father uses the near demonstrative οὗτος to single out Jesus at His baptism. The demonstrative points with divine authority at the Person of the Son
verse_connections.text: And lo a voice from heaven, saying, This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased

verse_connections.note: ἐκεῖνος — that One (the Spirit). Five times in John 14-16 Jesus uses ἐκεῖνος for the Spirit, consistently choosing the masculine demonstrative despite πνεῦμα being neuter. This is Jesus's own grammar lesson about the Spirit's personhood
verse_connections.text: He shall glorify me: for he shall receive of mine, and shall shew it unto you

grammar.title: Relative Pronouns
grammar.subtitle: ὅς ἥ ὅ — who, which, that — Intermediate

rows: εὐαγγέλιον is neuter → ὅ is neuter
Case comes from role in clause
ὅ is object of εὐηγγελισάμην → accusative
tables.title: Rules for Relative Pronouns

examples.note: ὅς is masculine nominative — subject of θέλῃ within the clause. The antecedent is implied: that person who...
examples.greek: ὃς γὰρ ἐὰν θέλῃ τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ σῶσαι
examples.translation: For whosoever will save his life

examples.note: ὅ is neuter accusative — object of εὐηγγελισάμην within the clause. Agrees with εὐαγγέλιον (neuter) in gender
examples.translation: the gospel which I preached to you

examples.note: Relative clause with participle. The grace (feminine) which was given — gender agreement with χάρις
examples.translation: the grace that was given to me

content.overview: Relative pronouns introduce relative clauses — subordinate clauses that describe or identify a noun (the antecedent). In English: the man who came, the word that was spoken, the grace which saves. In Greek, the relative pronoun must agree with its antecedent in GENDER and NUMBER, but takes its CASE from its function within the relative clause — a key distinction from English.

practice.answer: From its grammatical function within the relative clause itself
practice.question: A relative pronoun agrees with its antecedent in gender and number. From where does it get its case?

options: Because it is the object of preaching
Because relative pronouns are always neuter
practice.question: In τὸ εὐαγγέλιον ὃ εὐηγγελισάμην (the gospel which I preached), why is ὅ neuter?

options: Because relative pronouns are always accusative
Because ὅ is the direct object of εὐηγγελισάμην within the relative clause
practice.question: In the same phrase, why is ὅ accusative?

key_points: ὅς = who/which/that (masculine); ἥ = who/which/that (feminine); ὅ = which/that (neuter)
Relative pronoun AGREES with antecedent in gender and number
Relative pronoun takes its CASE from its role WITHIN the clause (not from the antecedent)
This gender/number agreement + case from function is the key to parsing relative pronouns
Attraction: sometimes the relative is pulled into the case of the antecedent (attraction)
Key NT relative clauses: whosoever (πᾶς ὅς = all who), which (ὅ = that which)

verse_connections.note: τὸ εὐαγγέλιον ὃ εὐηγγελισάμην — the gospel which I preached. Two relative clauses stack up to define the gospel Paul is defending. The relative pronouns identify and anchor the content
verse_connections.text: Moreover, brethren, I declare unto you the gospel which I preached unto you, which also ye have received, and wherein ye stand

verse_connections.note: ὃς δ' ἂν πίῃ — whoever drinks (relative with ἂν + subjunctive = indefinite relative clause). The open invitation of the relative whosoever echoes John 3:16. Christ's offer has no qualifier except willingness to drink
verse_connections.text: But whosoever drinketh of the water that I shall give him shall never thirst

grammar.title: The Subjunctive Mood
grammar.subtitle: Possibility, purpose & condition — Advanced

examples.note: ἵνα + subjunctive (ἔχῃ) — purpose clause. The purpose of the cross: that those who believe might have (subjunctive, ongoing) eternal life
examples.greek: ἵνα πᾶς ὁ πιστεύων εἰς αὐτὸν ἔχῃ ζωὴν αἰώνιον
examples.translation: in order that everyone believing in him might have eternal life

examples.note: ἐάν + aorist subjunctive (ἁμάρτῃ) — third class condition: a real possibility. John writes for the case that any believer sins
examples.translation: if anyone should sin

examples.note: 1st plural present subjunctive — hortatory subjunctive. A warm exhortation: let us keep on loving one another
examples.translation: let us love one another

content.overview: The Indicative mood states facts; the Subjunctive mood expresses possibility, probability, contingency, or the speaker's attitude. The Subjunctive is the mood of maybe, might, should, in order that. It uses lengthened thematic vowels (ο → ω; ε → η) and appears in purpose clauses (ἵνα + subjunctive), conditional sentences (ἐάν + subjunctive), prohibitions (μή + aorist subjunctive), and indefinite relative/temporal clauses (ἂν + relative + subjunctive).

practice.answer: Lengthened thematic vowels — ο becomes ω, ε becomes η (e.g. λύει indicative vs λύῃ subjunctive)
practice.question: What is the key grammatical signal that distinguishes the Subjunctive from the Indicative in Greek?

options: That everyone believing might have eternal life
practice.question: ἵνα + subjunctive expresses purpose. In John 3:16 the purpose clause is ἵνα...ἔχῃ ζωὴν αἰώνιον. What is the purpose of God giving His Son?

options: It is a real, genuinely possible future contingency
It is certainly going to happen
practice.question: ἐάν + subjunctive is a third class condition. What does this indicate about the condition?

key_points: Subjunctive = the mood of possibility, purpose, contingency
Distinguished from indicative by lengthened vowels: -ω, -ῃς, -ῃ, -ωμεν, -ητε, -ωσι
Purpose clause: ἵνα + subjunctive (in order that)
Condition: ἐάν + subjunctive (if — third class condition, real probability)
Prohibition: μή + aorist subjunctive (don't even start doing this)
Indefinite: ὅς ἐάν / ὅταν + subjunctive (whoever, whenever)
Hortatory subjunctive: 1st plural — let us love (ἀγαπῶμεν)

verse_connections.note: ἵνα μὴ ἀπόληται ἀλλ' ἔχῃ ζωὴν αἰώνιον — two subjunctives: ἀπόληται (perish, aorist subj, prevented by μή) and ἔχῃ (have, present subj, ongoing possession). The grammar of salvation in one purpose clause
verse_connections.text: that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life

verse_connections.note: ἐάν τις ἁμάρτῃ (ἐάν + aorist subjunctive) — the compassionate third class condition. John's pastoral realism: he doesn't pretend believers never sin. The subjunctive acknowledges the real possibility and immediately offers the remedy: an Advocate
verse_connections.text: My little children, these things write I unto you, that ye sin not. And if any man sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous

grammar.title: The Imperative Mood
grammar.subtitle: Commands & prohibitions — Advanced

rows: λύσατε (loose it now, all of you!)
tables.title: Aorist Active Imperative: λύσω

rows: You were already doing it; cease
tables.title: Prohibitions

examples.note: Present imperative — repentance is not a one-time act but an ongoing lifestyle and posture before God
examples.translation: Keep repenting! Repent continuously!

examples.note: Aorist imperative — the decisive, summary command. This is the one imperative in the Great Commission
examples.translation: Make disciples! (the decisive command)

examples.note: μή + present imperative = stop doing what you're already doing. The implication: someone was already looking back
examples.translation: Stop turning back / Do not keep turning

content.overview: The Imperative mood expresses commands, exhortations, requests, and prohibitions. Greek has imperatives in 2nd and 3rd person (English only uses 2nd person commands). Tense is crucial in imperatives: Present imperative = keep on doing (ongoing command); Aorist imperative = do it now, decisively (summary command). The distinction between present and aorist imperatives carries significant pastoral weight in the NT letters.

practice.answer: Present imperative = keep doing (ongoing habit); Aorist imperative = do it now decisively (summary command)
practice.question: What is the difference between a Present Imperative and an Aorist Imperative in terms of the kind of action commanded?

options: Keep on praying — make it a continuous, unceasing habit
practice.question: 1 Thessalonians 5:17 says Pray without ceasing (προσεύχεσθε ἀδιαλείπτως — present imperative). What does the present imperative communicate?

options: Don't ever begin this at all
practice.question: μή + present imperative means stop doing something. What does μή + aorist subjunctive mean?

key_points: Present imperative: ongoing, repeated, habitual command — keep doing this
Aorist imperative: decisive, summary command — do this now
Prohibition with μή + present imperative: stop doing this (you were already doing it)
Prohibition with μή + aorist subjunctive: don't even start (you weren't yet doing it)
3rd person imperative: let him..., let them... — used for indirect commands
The Great Commission (Matt 28:19) uses the aorist imperative for the main command
Pray without ceasing (1 Thess 5:17) is a present imperative — keep on praying

verse_connections.note: μαθητεύσατε — aorist imperative (decisive command: make disciples now and fully). The participles (going, baptizing, teaching) are ongoing present participles — the method is continuous; the mission is decisive
verse_connections.text: Go ye therefore, and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost

verse_connections.note: Three commands: χαίρετε (rejoice, present), προσεύχεσθε (pray, present), εὐχαριστεῖτε (give thanks, present) — all present imperatives. Paul's trinitarian formula for the Christian life: ongoing joy, ongoing prayer, ongoing gratitude
verse_connections.text: Rejoice evermore. Pray without ceasing. In every thing give thanks

grammar.title: Participles — Advanced
grammar.subtitle: Attributive, circumstantial & genitive absolute — Advanced

rows: ὁ ἀγαθὸς λόγος — the good word
πιστεύσας σωθήσεται — having believed he will be saved
tables.title: Three Main Participle Uses

rows: while/as (he does X, he does Y)
λέγων ἀπῆλθεν — saying this, he left
after/having done (X, he then did Y)
εἰπὼν ἀπῆλθεν — having said, he left
πεπιστευκὼς ζήσεται — having believed, he will live
tables.title: Circumstantial Participle Time Relationships

examples.note: Aorist participle εἰπών (having said) is prior to the main verb ἐπορεύθη (journeyed). The saying happened first, then the journey
examples.greek: εἰπὼν δὲ ταῦτα ἐπορεύθη εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα
examples.translation: Having said these things he journeyed to Jerusalem

examples.note: Genitive absolute — αὐτοῦ ἐλθόντος (him having come, genitive + genitive participle). Subject of the participle (αὐτοῦ) is different from main verb subject
examples.translation: When he had come into Capernaum

examples.note: Substantival present participle ὁ νικῶν = the overcomer (the one overcoming). Occurs 7 times in Revelation 2-3 as the defining description of the faithful Christian
examples.translation: The one who overcomes will inherit these things

content.overview: Beyond the basic introduction (Lesson 15), Greek participles have several advanced uses that are essential for NT exegesis. The three main uses are: (1) Attributive — modifies a noun like an adjective; (2) Substantival — functions as a noun itself; (3) Circumstantial — describes the circumstances of the main verb (when, while, because, although, if, by). Tense in circumstantial participles encodes timing and logical relationship to the main verb.

practice.answer: A participle and its subject, both in the genitive case, forming a clause grammatically independent from the main clause
practice.question: What is the genitive absolute construction in Greek?

options: The one who has already won
The one who keeps on overcoming / the overcomer
practice.question: ὁ νικῶν (present participle) in Revelation 2-3 functions as a substantival participle. What does it mean?

options: Prior (happening before the main verb's action)
practice.question: An AORIST circumstantial participle describes action that is ___ relative to the main verb.

key_points: Attributive: ὁ πιστεύων (the believing one) — with article, modifies noun
Circumstantial: action or state accompanying the main verb — time, cause, means, concession
Aorist circumstantial participle: action prior to main verb — having believed, he...
Present circumstantial participle: action simultaneous with main verb — while believing, he...
Genitive absolute: participle + subject in genitive case, grammatically independent of main clause
Redundant participle (pleonastic): ἀποκριθεὶς εἶπεν (answering, he said) — common Semitic idiom in NT

verse_connections.note: ὁ νικῶν — the overcoming one. Seven times in Revelation 2-3 this substantival present participle defines the faithful Christian. The ongoing nature of the present tense shows victory is a lifestyle, not a one-time event
verse_connections.text: He that overcometh shall inherit all things; and I will be his God, and he shall be my son

verse_connections.note: Εἰσελθόντι δὲ αὐτῷ — genitive absolute (him having entered). The centurion's faith story begins with a genitive absolute construction that sets the scene. The grammar of Matthew signals narrative transitions with this advanced construction
verse_connections.text: And when Jesus was entered into Capernaum, there came unto him a centurion, beseeching him

grammar.title: The Four Conditional Sentences
grammar.subtitle: If...then — Four classes — Advanced

examples.note: Satan uses a 1st class condition — he's not conceding Jesus is the Son; he's testing whether Jesus will use His power selfishly
examples.greek: εἰ υἱὸς εἶ τοῦ θεοῦ (1st class)
examples.translation: If you are the Son of God (and for argument's sake I'm granting it)...

examples.note: 2nd class contrary-to-fact: Martha knows Lazarus is dead. The grammar encodes her grief — the condition was NOT met in reality
examples.greek: εἰ ἦν ὧδε οὐκ ἂν ἀπέθανεν (2nd class)
examples.translation: If you had been here (but you weren't), my brother would not have died (but he did)

examples.note: 3rd class condition: Jesus opens the promise to any and all who choose to love Him — the subjunctive keeps it genuinely open
examples.translation: If anyone loves me (open question, genuine possibility)

content.overview: Greek has four distinct conditional sentence types (classes), each encoding the speaker's assumption about the truth or probability of the condition. The class affects the logical strength and nature of the if-then argument. Recognising which class a conditional belongs to is essential for NT exegesis — it tells you whether Paul assumes his condition is true, possible, unlikely, or contrary to fact.

practice.answer: 1st class: condition assumed as true for the argument (may or may not be actually true). 2nd class: condition assumed to be CONTRARY TO FACT — the condition was not met.
practice.question: What is the key difference between a 1st class and a 2nd class conditional sentence?

options: She thinks Jesus could still help
She is stating that the condition WAS NOT met — Lazarus is genuinely dead, and she knows it
She is uncertain whether Lazarus is dead
practice.question: John 11:21 — If you had been here, my brother would not have died (2nd class conditional). What does this tell us about Martha's understanding of the situation?

options: Belief is predetermined with no genuine choice
The offer is genuinely open to all — a real possibility for any person
practice.question: ἐάν + subjunctive is a 3rd class condition. In John 3:16 (whosoever believeth), what does the open nature of ἐάν communicate?

key_points: First Class: εἰ + indicative (any tense). Assumed true for argument's sake. If, and for argument I'll grant it (whether true or not).
Second Class: εἰ + past indicative + ἄν + past indicative. Contrary to fact. If it were so (but it's not)
Third Class: ἐάν + subjunctive + indicative. Future probability. If (should) this happen (genuine open question)
Fourth Class: εἰ + optative (rare, incomplete in NT). Remote future possibility. If ever this might happen
The protasis is the if-clause; the apodosis is the then-clause
First class conditions do NOT guarantee the truth of the condition — they grant it for argument

verse_connections.note: εἰ ἦς ὧδε, οὐκ ἂν ἀπέθανεν — 2nd class contrary-to-fact condition. The grammar carries Martha's grief: the condition was NOT met. Lazarus IS dead. Her faith and her loss collide in this conditional sentence
verse_connections.text: Then said Martha unto Jesus, Lord, if thou hadst been here, my brother had not died

verse_connections.note: εἰ υἱὸς εἶ — 1st class condition. Satan doesn't deny Jesus is the Son; he tests whether Jesus will use His identity to satisfy self-interest. The 1st class grammar is Satan's subtle acknowledgment while pressing the temptation
verse_connections.text: And when the tempter came to him, he said, If thou be the Son of God, command that these stones be made bread

grammar.title: Indirect Discourse
grammar.subtitle: Reporting what was said or thought — Advanced

rows: λέγει ὅτι ζῇ (he says that he lives)
κελεύει αὐτὸν ἐλθεῖν (he commands him to come)
ὁρῶ αὐτὸν ἐρχόμενον (I see him coming)
tables.title: Three Methods of Indirect Discourse

examples.note: πιστεύω + ὅτι + indicative — indirect discourse stating the content of Martha's belief. The present tense εἶ is retained from her conviction
examples.greek: πιστεύω ὅτι σὺ εἶ ὁ Χριστός
examples.translation: I believe that you are the Christ

examples.note: Historical present (λέγει) + ὅτι recitative — here functioning as quotation marks for the direct speech that follows
examples.greek: λέγει αὐτοῖς ὁ Ἰησοῦς· Ἐγώ εἰμι
examples.translation: Jesus says to them: I am (he)

examples.note: οἴδαμεν + ὅτι + present indicative — indirect discourse reporting shared theological conviction. The present tense is retained from the original reality
examples.greek: οἴδαμεν ὅτι ὁ νόμος πνευματικός ἐστιν
examples.translation: We know that the law is spiritual

content.overview: Indirect discourse reports the content of someone's speech or thought indirectly (He said that... rather than He said: ...). Greek has three main methods of indirect discourse: (1) ὅτι + indicative verb (most common — retains original tense of the direct statement); (2) Infinitive construction (with verbs of commanding, wishing, believing); (3) Participle construction (with verbs of perceiving, knowing). Understanding which method applies — and the tense retention rules — is crucial for NT exegesis.

practice.answer: ὅτι + indicative verb, retaining the original tense of the reported speech
practice.question: What is the most common method of indirect discourse in Greek NT?

options: Martha's confession: I believe that You are the Christ — the content of her faith
practice.question: John 11:27 — πιστεύω ὅτι σὺ εἶ ὁ Χριστός. This is indirect discourse. What belief is being reported?

options: The cause of what was just said
The content of what we know — that the law is spiritual
practice.question: In Romans 7:14, οἴδαμεν ὅτι ὁ νόμος πνευματικός ἐστιν — what does the ὅτι introduce here?

key_points: Method 1: ὅτι + indicative — tense of indirect clause = tense of original speech
Method 2: Infinitive — with verbs of commanding (κελεύω), wishing (βούλομαι), believing (πιστεύω)
Method 3: Participle — with verbs of knowing (οἶδα), seeing (ὁράω), finding (εὑρίσκω)
Tense retention: the original speaker's tense is preserved in ὅτι clauses (historic present, aorist, etc.)
The ὅτι recitative introduces direct quotation (like English quotation marks): He said, that = He said:
Context determines whether ὅτι is because (causal) or that (content)

verse_connections.note: πεπίστευκα ὅτι σύ εἶ ὁ Χριστός (perfect + ὅτι + indicative) — I have believed and continue to believe (perfect) that you are (present, retained) the Christ. Martha's indirect discourse confession equals Peter's in theological weight
verse_connections.text: She saith unto him, Yea, Lord: I believe that thou art the Christ, the Son of God, which should come into the world

verse_connections.note: οἴδαμεν ὅτι — we know that (indirect discourse, common Pauline formula). Paul uses this construction repeatedly in Romans to signal shared theological premises before building his argument. Knowing the indirect discourse marker helps you follow Paul's logic
verse_connections.text: For we know that the law is spiritual: but I am carnal, sold under sin

grammar.title: The Genitive Absolute
grammar.subtitle: Independent participial clause — Advanced

examples.note: αὐτοῦ πορευομένου — genitive absolute (him while going). Subject of genitive absolute = αὐτοῦ (him). Subject of main verb = οἱ ὄχλοι (the crowds). Different subjects — correctly an absolute
examples.greek: αὐτοῦ δὲ πορευομένου ἐθαύμασαν οἱ ὄχλοι
examples.translation: While he was going, the crowds marvelled

examples.note: Aorist genitive absolute — αὐτοῦ ἐλθόντος (him having come). Used to introduce a new scene. Note: different from the disciples who are the implied background
examples.translation: When he had come into Capernaum

examples.note: Aorist genitive absolute — αὐτοῦ εἰπόντος (him having spoken). The speaking is prior to and precipitates the believing. Classic narrative transition
examples.greek: ταῦτα δὲ αὐτοῦ εἰπόντος πολλοὶ ἐπίστευσαν
examples.translation: And when he had said these things, many believed

content.overview: The genitive absolute is a grammatically independent participial clause in which both the participle and its subject stand in the genitive case, while having no grammatical connection to the main clause. The subject of the genitive absolute is DIFFERENT from the subject of the main verb — that is what makes it absolute (unattached). This construction is frequently used in NT narrative to set the scene for a main clause, functioning like a temporal, causal, or circumstantial clause.

practice.answer: The subject of the participial clause is DIFFERENT from the subject of the main clause — both the subject and participle stand in the genitive case
practice.question: What makes a participial clause a genuine genitive absolute?

options: Both αὐτοῦ (pronoun) and ἐλθόντος (participle) are in the genitive case, and Jesus is different from the centurion who approaches
The verb ἐλθόντος is in the accusative case
The main verb is also in the genitive
αὐτοῦ is the subject of the main verb
practice.question: In Matthew 8:5, ἐλθόντος δὲ αὐτοῦ (him having come) is a genitive absolute. What clues identify it?
An aorist genitive absolute participle indicates its action is ___ to the main verb.

key_points: Genitive absolute = participiple in genitive + its subject in genitive, grammatically independent
Subject of genitive absolute is DIFFERENT from main clause subject
Most often temporal: when he had..., while they were...
Can be causal: because he had said..., or concessive: although they knew...
Extremely common in narrative sections of Gospels and Acts
Identified by: genitive noun/pronoun + genitive participle at the start of a sentence
The participle tense shows relationship to main verb: aorist = prior; present = simultaneous

verse_connections.note: Εἰσελθόντι δὲ αὐτῷ — strictly a dative absolute here (him having entered, dative). Matthew uses participial absolute clauses as scene-setting transitions throughout his Gospel. Recognising these constructions reveals Matthew's narrative architecture
verse_connections.text: And when Jesus was entered into Capernaum, there came unto him a centurion, beseeching him

verse_connections.note: Καὶ ἐν τῷ συμπληροῦσθαι τὴν ἡμέραν τῆς πεντηκοστῆς — an articular infinitive construction (in the completing of the day) used where a genitive absolute might appear. Luke's sophisticated Greek shows the variety of ways Greek sets temporal scenes
verse_connections.text: And when the day of Pentecost was fully come, they were all with one accord in one place

grammar.title: The Article as Pronoun
grammar.subtitle: ὁ δέ, οἱ δέ — Advanced uses of the article — Advanced

rows: Both nouns refer to the SAME person
tables.title: Granville Sharp's Rule (simplified)

examples.note: ὁ δέ = Jesus (subject change from the tempter). The article as pronoun rapidly shifts subject back to Jesus without repeating His name
examples.translation: But he, answering, said

examples.note: οἱ δέ = the apostles (subject change from the women). The article-as-pronoun tracks the different groups' reactions in the resurrection narrative
examples.translation: But they said

examples.note: Granville Sharp's Rule: one article governs both θεοῦ and σωτῆρος — they refer to the same Person: Jesus Christ is God and Saviour
examples.greek: θεοῦ καὶ σωτῆρος ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ
examples.translation: of our God and Saviour Jesus Christ

content.overview: The Greek definite article originally functioned as a demonstrative pronoun (this/that), and this original function survives in the NT, especially in narrative. The construction Article + δέ (= he, she, they — the one/ones just mentioned) is extremely common in the Gospels and Acts for quick subject changes without repeating the noun. Understanding this use helps readers track who is speaking or acting in rapid-dialogue passages.

practice.answer: But he / and he — it marks a subject change and refers back to the most recently mentioned male person
practice.question: What does the construction ὁ δέ mean when used as a pronoun in NT narrative?

options: Functioning as a pronoun (he) with a subject-change marker (δέ)
practice.question: In Matthew's Gospel, ὁ δέ... εἶπεν appears frequently. What grammatical function is the article performing?

options: Two different persons (God the Father and Jesus)
The same single person — Jesus Christ is identified as both God and Saviour
practice.question: Granville Sharp's Rule (Titus 2:13: our great God and Saviour Jesus Christ) shows that the one article governing both nouns means they refer to:

key_points: Article + δέ = he/she/they (subject change marker): ὁ δέ = but he; οἱ δέ = but they
Used most commonly in narrative when subject changes from one person to another
The article acts as a pronoun referring back to a recently mentioned person/group
Article + μέν...ὁ δέ = on the one hand...on the other hand (contrast)
The article can also substantivize any word: ὁ ἐρχόμενος = the coming one, the one who comes
Granville Sharp's Rule: article + noun + καί + noun (no second article) = same person — applies to Titus 2:13 (God and Saviour = one person)

verse_connections.note: τοῦ μεγάλου θεοῦ καὶ σωτῆρος ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ — one article governs both θεοῦ and σωτῆρος. Granville Sharp's Rule: they refer to the same Person. This verse is one of the strongest direct statements of Christ's deity in the NT
verse_connections.text: Looking for that blessed hope, and the glorious appearing of the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ

verse_connections.note: οἱ δέ — but they (the apostles). The article-as-pronoun tracks the subject shift from the women (who reported) to the apostles (who disbelieved). Luke uses this construction skillfully to show the tragic irony of Easter morning
verse_connections.text: And their words seemed to them as idle tales, and they believed them not

grammar.title: Greek Word Order & Emphasis
grammar.subtitle: Position drives meaning — Advanced

rows: θεός fronted — deity of the Word emphasized
tables.title: Rhetorically Significant Word Orders in John 1:1

examples.note: ἐγώ stands first, highly emphatic. The explicit personal pronoun (normally omitted) placed first signals the I AM declaration — divine self-identification before the claim
examples.greek: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ ἀνάστασις καὶ ἡ ζωή
examples.translation: I AM the resurrection and the life

examples.note: An alternative word order for John 3:16 where κόσμος is fronted. The actual Greek places οὕτως (so/thus) first — emphasising the manner of God's love
examples.translation: The world — God loved it (emphasis on world)

examples.note: ἐν ἀρχῇ is placed first — the temporal setting is emphatic, deliberately echoing Genesis 1:1. John puts the eternal origin in first position to anchor everything that follows
examples.translation: In the beginning was the Word

content.overview: Unlike English (where word order is relatively fixed and grammatically necessary), Greek word order is relatively free because CASE ENDINGS indicate grammatical function. This freedom means word order in Greek is used primarily for EMPHASIS and RHETORIC rather than grammar. The most emphatic positions in a Greek clause are the BEGINNING (initial emphasis) and the END (final emphasis). The beginning of a sentence is the place of greatest prominence — placing a word first is a deliberate choice.

practice.answer: Because Greek case endings already show grammatical function (subject, object, etc.), so word order is free to carry rhetorical emphasis
practice.question: Why does Greek word order communicate emphasis rather than grammatical function?

options: John emphasises the divine nature of the Word by placing θεός first (predicate emphasis — this is what the Word IS)
θεός is the object of the verb
practice.question: In John 1:1, θεὸς ἦν ὁ λόγος — the word God (θεός) is placed FIRST in its clause. What does this forward placement communicate?

options: The explicit first-position ἐγώ is maximally emphatic — I and no one else am this thing
ἐγώ is required for the verb εἰμι
It is a first century idiom with no special meaning
practice.question: In the I AM statements of Jesus (ἐγώ εἰμι), the explicit pronoun ἐγώ is placed first. What does this communicate?

key_points: Greek word order is driven by emphasis, not grammar (cases handle function)
First position in a clause = most emphatic (marked, forward-moved element)
Last position can also carry emphasis (anticlimax reversal, climactic placement)
Normal SOV order (Subject-Object-Verb) is the baseline; departures from this are marked
Verbs often come last in Greek, so a verb moved first is very emphatic
Adjectives and genitives moved before their nouns carry forward emphasis
Postpositive words (δέ, γάρ, οὖν) are never first — they claim second position by rule

verse_connections.note: Three clauses, each with significant word order choices: ἐν ἀρχῇ fronted (eternity), πρὸς τὸν θεόν (relationship), θεὸς ἦν (deity with θεός fronted). John's entire prologue is a masterclass in Greek emphasis through word order
verse_connections.text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God

verse_connections.note: ἐγώ εἰμι — I AM. The explicit ἐγώ fronted before εἰμι is not grammatically required (εἰμι already encodes 1st person) but rhetorically explosive. In the shadow of Lazarus' tomb, the source of resurrection places Himself in first position
verse_connections.text: I am the resurrection, and the life: he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live

grammar.title: Textual Criticism Basics
grammar.subtitle: How we got our Greek NT — Advanced

rows: Luke and John — key Alexandrian witness
Complete NT — found at Sinai by Tischendorf
Most complete early manuscript — Vatican Library
Basis of KJV NT — primarily Byzantine text
tables.title: Major Manuscript Witnesses

rows: Less time for errors to accumulate
A difficult reading is less likely to be invented
Additions easier to explain than deletions
Vocabulary, theology, syntax of the author
tables.title: Textual Criticism Principles (canons)

examples.note: This verse appears in some early manuscripts but is absent from others (Papyrus 75, Codex B). Square brackets in critical editions signal textual uncertainty. Its absence from major Alexandrian texts raises questions; its content perfectly fits Luke's theology
examples.greek: [ὁ δὲ Ἰησοῦς ἔλεγεν, Πάτερ, ἄφες αὐτοῖς, οὐ γὰρ οἴδασιν τί ποιοῦσιν]
examples.translation: [But Jesus was saying, Father, forgive them; for they do not know what they are doing]

examples.note: Well-attested passage showing John forbidding Jesus — present in all major manuscripts. Contrast with verses in square brackets
examples.translation: [And John was forbidding him saying...]

examples.note: Well-attested personal name showing a stable portion of the text — the vast majority of NT text is not disputed at all
examples.translation: Mary Magdalene

content.overview: The NT was composed in Greek in the 1st century AD, but we do not have the original manuscripts (autographs). We have approximately 5,800 Greek manuscripts — more than any other ancient document by a massive margin. Textual criticism is the science of comparing these manuscripts to reconstruct the original text. Understanding the basics helps students evaluate why English translations sometimes differ and why study notes say some manuscripts omit or add a reading.

practice.answer: Approximately 5,800 — more manuscript evidence than any other ancient document
practice.question: How many Greek New Testament manuscripts are known to exist?
The oldest surviving NT manuscript fragment is Papyrus 52 (𝔓⁵²), dating to approximately AD 125. Which NT book does it contain?
The KJV NT was translated from the Textus Receptus (Erasmus, 1516). What manuscript family does the Textus Receptus primarily represent?

key_points: 5,800+ Greek NT manuscripts (papyri, uncials, minuscules, lectionaries)
No two manuscripts are identical — minor variations exist throughout
Textual variants: most are minor (spelling, word order); very few affect doctrine
Key manuscript families: Alexandrian (oldest, best quality — e.g. Codex Vaticanus B, Sinaiticus א), Byzantine (majority — basis of Textus Receptus/KJV)
Key papyri: 𝔓⁴⁵ (Chester Beatty, ~AD 250), 𝔓⁷⁵ (AD 175-225), 𝔓⁵² (oldest NT fragment, ~AD 125 — John 18)
Principles: prefer shorter reading, harder reading, reading that explains the others, older witnesses

verse_connections.note: This passage is preserved in the oldest NT fragment, 𝔓⁵² (John Rylands Papyrus), dated ~AD 125 — within 30 years of the Gospel's composition. Holding that fragment, you are 30 years from the apostle John himself. The text we read is historically reliable
verse_connections.text: Then said Pilate unto them, Take ye him, and judge him according to your law. The Jews therefore said unto him, It is not lawful for us to put any man to death

verse_connections.note: The Comma Johanneum — these specific words (Father, Word, Holy Ghost) are absent from all Greek manuscripts before AD 1500, from all early translations, and from all early church fathers. Most modern translations bracket or omit them. Textual criticism protects doctrine by excluding later additions, not undermining it
verse_connections.text: For there are three that bear record in heaven, the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost: and these three are one

vocabulary.title: θεός — God
vocabulary.subtitle: G2316 · 1,317 occurrences · The primary name for God

practice.question: What does θεός (theos) mean?
How many times does θεός appear in the New Testament?
In John 20:28, Thomas declares My Lord and my God! The word God is θεός. Who is Thomas calling θεός?

content.etymology: Related to the Proto-Indo-European root dhēs meaning divine. Greeks used it for any deity; the NT writers use it overwhelmingly for the one true God of Israel.
related_words: θεότης (theotēs, G2320) — deity, the fullness of divine nature (Col 2:9)
content.theological_significance: The most common word for God in the NT appearing 1317 times. Used for all three persons of the Trinity. NT writers also use it to affirm Jesus as divine (John 1:1, John 20:28, Titus 2:13). The compound Immanuel means God (El) with us.

verse_connections.note: θεός appears twice — once for the Father (with God) and once as a predicate for Christ (was God). The most contested and profound theological claim of the NT
verse_connections.text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God

verse_connections.note: θεός ἐστιν ἀγάπη — God IS love. Not merely that God loves, but that love is His very nature. θεός defines ἀγάπη and ἀγάπη defines θεός
verse_connections.text: He that loveth not knoweth not God; for God is love

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: For in him dwelleth all the fulness of the Godhead bodily
All scripture is given by inspiration of God

vocabulary.title: κύριος — Lord
vocabulary.subtitle: G2962 · 748 occurrences · The earliest Christian confession

practice.question: What does κύριος (kyrios) mean?
The earliest Christian confession was κύριος Ἰησοῦς — what does this mean?
In the Greek OT (Septuagint), κύριος translates which Hebrew divine name?

content.etymology: From kyros meaning authority or power. In the Septuagint (Greek OT), κύριος translates the divine name YHWH — so when NT authors call Jesus κύριος they are making an unmistakable claim to His deity.
related_words: κυριεύω (kyrieuō, G2961) — to be lord over, to rule
content.theological_significance: The earliest Christian creed was three words: κύριος Ἰησοῦς — Jesus is Lord (Rom 10:9). By applying the Septuagint's translation of YHWH (κύριος) to Jesus, the NT writers declare His full divinity. Phil 2:9-11 uses Isa 45:23 (an YHWH passage) to proclaim Jesus as κύριος.

verse_connections.note: κύριος Ἰησοῦς — the saving confession. Paul links the mouth-confession of Christ's Lordship with heart-belief in His resurrection
verse_connections.text: That if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in thine heart that God hath raised him from the dead, thou shalt be saved

verse_connections.note: The cosmic conclusion: every tongue will confess κύριος — the name above every name applied to Jesus. Quoting Isaiah 45:23 (an YHWH passage) and applying it to Christ
verse_connections.text: And that every tongue should confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father

_vocabulary.meaning: to be lord over / to rule / to master
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: For sin shall not have dominion over you: for ye are not under the law
Far above all principality, and power, and might, and dominion

practice.question: What does Χριστός (Christos) mean?
Χριστός is the Greek translation of which Hebrew word?

options: Kings, Priests, and Prophets — all three roles Jesus fulfils
practice.question: The word Christos comes from chriō meaning to anoint. In the OT, who was anointed with oil?

content.etymology: The Greek translation of the Hebrew Mashiach (Messiah). Christos comes from chriō meaning to anoint — used when oil was poured over a king, priest, or prophet to consecrate them. Jesus is the ultimate fulfilment of all three roles: Prophet, Priest, and King.
related_words: ἀντίχριστος (antichristos, G500) — antichrist, one opposing the anointed (1 John 2:18)
content.theological_significance: Originally used as a title (the Christ = the Messiah) it quickly became part of Jesus' personal name (Jesus Christ). The entire OT pointed toward the Χριστός. When Peter confesses Thou art the Christ (Matt 16:16), he uses this word. The X in Xmas is Chi — the first letter of Χριστός.

verse_connections.note: Peter's great confession: σὺ εἶ ὁ Χριστός — You are the Anointed One. Jesus calls this revelation a foundation stone of the church
verse_connections.text: And Simon Peter answered and said, Thou art the Christ, the Son of the living God

verse_connections.note: κύριον καὶ Χριστόν — Lord AND Christ. Peter's Pentecost declaration combining both great titles for the risen Jesus
verse_connections.text: Therefore let all the house of Israel know assuredly, that God hath made that same Jesus, whom ye have crucified, both Lord and Christ

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he hath anointed me to preach
Ye have heard that antichrist shall come

content.meaning: Jesus (Hebrew: Yeshua — God saves / the LORD is salvation)

practice.question: What does the name Ἰησοῦς (Iēsous / Jesus) mean in Hebrew?
The name Jesus (Ἰησοῦς) is the Greek form of which Hebrew name?

options: For he shall save his people from their sins
practice.question: The angel told Mary and Joseph to name the child Jesus. Why?

content.etymology: Greek form of the Hebrew Yehoshua (Joshua) meaning YHWH saves. The angel commanded this specific name: thou shalt call his name JESUS: for he shall save his people from their sins (Matt 1:21). The name is itself a declaration of His mission.
related_words: σωτήρ (sōtēr, G4990) — Saviour, the one who saves
σωτηρία (sōtēria, G4991) — salvation, the act and result of saving
content.theological_significance: The most frequent proper name in the NT at 975 occurrences. His name is literally salvation — every time someone called His name they were proclaiming the gospel. There is no other name under heaven given among men, whereby we must be saved (Acts 4:12). The name above every name (Phil 2:9).

verse_connections.note: The name Ἰησοῦς is given with its meaning attached. His identity and His mission are fused into one name — God saves
verse_connections.text: And she shall bring forth a son, and thou shalt call his name JESUS: for he shall save his people from their sins

verse_connections.note: The name (ὄνομα) of Ἰησοῦς is the only vehicle of salvation. Exclusivity rooted not in arrogance but in the uniqueness of God incarnate
verse_connections.text: Neither is there salvation in any other: for there is none other name under heaven given among men, whereby we must be saved

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: For unto you is born this day in the city of David a Saviour, which is Christ the Lord

_vocabulary.meaning: to save / to rescue / to heal / to keep safe
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: He shall save his people from their sins

practice.question: What does πνεῦμα (pneuma) mean?
In John 3:8 Jesus says the wind (πνεῦμα) blows where it will — He is using this as a picture of what?
Our English medical word pneumonia comes from πνεῦμα. What does it relate to?

content.etymology: From pneō meaning to breathe or to blow. The same word covers the physical breath, the wind, the human spirit, and the Holy Spirit — because the biblical authors see all of these as interconnected. God breathed (enephysēsen) into Adam and he became a living soul (Gen 2:7 LXX). Our words pneumatic and pneumonia come from this root.
related_words: πνέω (pneō, G4154) — to blow, to breathe
content.theological_significance: Used 385 times in the NT — for the Holy Spirit, for human spirits, for evil spirits, and for breath/wind (John 3:8). The pneuma is the animating, life-giving principle that connects the human to the divine. Crucially, God is Spirit (πνεῦμα ὁ θεός, John 4:24) — not bound by physical form.

verse_connections.note: πνεῦμα ὁ θεός — God is Spirit. This declaration shapes all true worship: not tied to a mountain or a temple but to the Spirit and truth
verse_connections.text: God is a Spirit: and they that worship him must worship him in spirit and in truth

verse_connections.note: The wind (πνοή, from same root as πνεῦμα) and the Spirit come together at Pentecost. The breath of God fills His people just as He breathed life into Adam
verse_connections.text: And suddenly there came a sound from heaven as of a rushing mighty wind...And they were all filled with the Holy Ghost

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: But the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God
The wind bloweth where it listeth, and thou hearest the sound thereof

practice.question: What does ἀγάπη (agapē) mean?
1 John 4:8 says God is ___. Fill in the Greek word.
Greek has three main words for love. Agapē is self-giving love. Philia is friendship. What is eros?

content.etymology: Rarely used in classical Greek before the NT. The NT writers essentially redefined and elevated this word to describe a uniquely divine love. Greek also had eros (passionate desire) and philia (friendship/affection) — agapē is the love that gives regardless of the worth of the recipient.
related_words: φιλέω (phileō, G5368) — to love as a friend / to have affection for (25×)
content.theological_significance: God IS ἀγάπη (1 John 4:8). The greatest commandment is to love (ἀγαπάω) God and neighbour (Matt 22:37-39). The love chapter (1 Cor 13) defines ἀγάπη by what it does and does not do. John 3:16 is the ἀγάπη of God in action — giving the most precious gift at the greatest cost for the least deserving.

verse_connections.note: ἠγάπησεν — the verb form of ἀγάπη. God's love is not a feeling but an action: He GAVE. The greatest definition of agapē is a cross.
verse_connections.text: For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son

verse_connections.note: Charity here is ἀγάπη. Paul's entire description is active — agapē does things and refuses other things. It is a choice, not merely an emotion
verse_connections.text: Charity suffereth long, and is kind; charity envieth not; charity vaunteth not itself

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: To all that be in Rome, beloved of God, called to be saints
Be kindly affectioned one to another with brotherly love

vocabulary.title: πίστις — Faith
vocabulary.subtitle: G4102 · 244 occurrences · The foundation of salvation

practice.question: What does πίστις (pistis) mean?
Hebrews 11:1 defines πίστις as the ___ of things hoped for.
Paul quotes Habakkuk 2:4 three times: The just shall live by ___. What word fills the blank?

content.etymology: From peithō meaning to persuade or to be convinced. Faith in the NT is not blind belief but a conviction that has been persuaded by evidence — the resurrection, the witness of Scripture, the testimony of changed lives.
related_words: πιστεύω (pisteuō, G4100) — to believe, to trust (248×)
content.theological_significance: The central word of Paul's theology. The just shall live by πίστις (Rom 1:17, Gal 3:11, Heb 10:38) — quoted from Habakkuk 2:4. We are saved through πίστις not works (Eph 2:8). The great faith chapter (Heb 11) defines πίστις as the substance of things hoped for — it has real substance. Also means faithfulness (as in God's faithfulness).

verse_connections.note: Saved (σεσῳσμένοι) through πίστις — faith is the channel, grace is the source. Both are gifts; neither is a human achievement
verse_connections.text: For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God

verse_connections.note: πίστις is ὑπόστασις (substance, title-deed) and ἔλεγχος (evidence, proof). Biblical faith is not feeling — it is the legal assurance of a future reality
verse_connections.text: Now faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: God is faithful, by whom ye were called unto the fellowship of his Son Jesus Christ
Be not faithless, but believing

practice.question: What does χάρις (charis) mean?
χάρισμα (charisma) comes from χάρις (grace). What does charisma mean in the NT?
Paul says in Romans 11:6 that if salvation is by grace, it cannot be by ___. These two are mutually exclusive.

content.etymology: From chairō meaning to rejoice, to be glad. Grace produces joy — both in the giver and receiver. The classical Greek sense was charm or attractiveness; the NT transforms it to mean unmerited divine favour given freely to the undeserving.
related_words: χαρίζομαι (charizomai, G5483) — to give freely, to forgive (23×)
χάρισμα (charisma, G5486) — gift of grace, spiritual gift (17×)
εὐχαριστία (eucharistia, G2169) — thanksgiving (15×, root of eucharist)
content.theological_significance: The word that defines the NT age. Every Pauline letter opens and closes with χάρις. Grace is not merely forgiveness — it is the active, life-giving power of God working in and through believers (1 Cor 15:10). By grace we are saved (Eph 2:5,8). Grace and works are mutually exclusive as the basis of salvation (Rom 11:6).

verse_connections.note: χάρις — the word that changes everything. Salvation is χάρις (grace) through πίστις (faith), not ἔργων (works). God's undeserved gift cannot be earned
verse_connections.text: For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God: not of works, lest any man should boast

verse_connections.note: χάρις καὶ ἀλήθεια — grace and truth together in the Person of Christ. The law defined the standard; grace provides the power to meet it through Christ
verse_connections.text: For the law was given by Moses, but grace and truth came by Jesus Christ

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Having then gifts differing according to the grace that is given to us
In every thing by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving let your requests be made known unto God

practice.question: What does σωτηρία (sōtēria) mean?

options: His very name declares His mission: salvation
practice.question: The name Jesus (Ἰησοῦς) comes from the Hebrew Yeshua which shares the same root as σωτηρία. What does this tell us about Jesus?

options: Present: sanctified (saved from power of sin)
Future: glorified (saved from presence of sin)
practice.question: Salvation (σωτηρία) in the NT has three tenses. Which is NOT one of them?

content.etymology: From sōzō (to save, to heal, to make whole) and sōtēr (saviour). The Greek concept included rescue from danger, healing of sickness, and political deliverance. The NT combines all these: Christ saves from sin (ultimate), heals the sick (temporal sign), and delivers from every enemy.
related_words: σῴζω (sōzō, G4982) — to save, to heal, to rescue (106×)
content.theological_significance: The grand theme of the entire Bible. The Hebrew equivalent is Yeshua — which is the personal name of Jesus. Salvation in the NT has three tenses: we HAVE BEEN saved (justification, past), we ARE BEING saved (sanctification, present), we WILL BE saved (glorification, future). It is a complete rescue operation covering past guilt, present power, and future glory.

verse_connections.note: σωτηρία is exclusive — one source, one name, one way. Not arrogance but the uniqueness of God's own rescue plan in Christ
verse_connections.text: Neither is there salvation in any other: for there is none other name under heaven given among men, whereby we must be saved

verse_connections.note: σωτηρία is God's δύναμις (power) unleashed — to the Jew first and also to the Greek. The gospel is the only sufficient power for complete salvation
verse_connections.text: For I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ: for it is the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: For the grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared to all men

_vocabulary.meaning: to bring safely through / to rescue completely
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And so it came to pass, that they escaped all safe to land

content.meaning: Sin / missing the mark / moral failure

practice.answer: Sin — from the archery term meaning to miss the mark
practice.question: What does ἁμαρτία (hamartia) mean — and what image does it come from?
Romans 3:23 says all have sinned and fall short of the ___ of God.

options: That we might become the righteousness of God in him
practice.question: In 2 Corinthians 5:21, Paul says God made Christ to be ἁμαρτία (sin) for us. What was the purpose?

content.etymology: From hamartenō meaning to miss the target — the word used in archery when an arrow misses its mark. Sin is not primarily rule-breaking but failing to hit God's intended purpose and design for human life. It is falling short of the glory of God (Rom 3:23).
related_words: ἁμαρτάνω (hamartanō, G264) — to sin, to miss the mark (43×)
παράπτωμα (paraptōma, G3900) — trespass, falling beside the path (19×)
content.theological_significance: The problem that makes salvation necessary. All have sinned (ἥμαρτον) and fall short of the glory of God (Rom 3:23). Sin entered through one man Adam (Rom 5:12). The solution is the Lamb of God who takes away the ἁμαρτία of the world (John 1:29). Christ was made ἁμαρτία for us (2 Cor 5:21) — the great exchange.

verse_connections.note: ἁμαρτία τοῦ κόσμου — the sin of the world. Christ came to remove the entire weight of human moral failure from the record and from the soul
verse_connections.text: Behold the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world

verse_connections.note: ἁμαρτίας — sins. The promise is dual: forgiveness (legal standing) AND cleansing (inner reality). Both dimensions of the sin problem are addressed
verse_connections.text: If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins, and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness

_vocabulary.meaning: sinner / one who misses the mark
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Joy shall be in heaven over one sinner that repenteth
And you hath he quickened, who were dead in trespasses and sins

practice.question: What does ἐκκλησία (ekklēsia) mean?

options: It is a group of people called out by God
practice.question: ἐκκλησία comes from ek (out) + kaleō (to call). What does this tell us about the church?
Jesus said I will build my ἐκκλησία. What did He promise the gates of hell would not do?

content.etymology: From ek (out of) + kaleō (to call). In Greek civic life, the ekklēsia was the public assembly of citizens called out from their homes to conduct the city's business. The NT takes this political term and fills it with spiritual meaning: the church is those called out of the world by God to conduct kingdom business.
related_words: καλέω (kaleō, G2564) — to call (148×, root of ekklēsia)
content.theological_significance: Jesus declared I will build my ἐκκλησία (Matt 16:18) — the only direct statement of Jesus about founding the church. The church is called out but also called together: not merely individual Christians but a covenant community. The church is the body of Christ (1 Cor 12:27), the bride of Christ (Eph 5:25-27), and the household of God (1 Tim 3:15).

verse_connections.note: ἐκκλησία — the only direct statement of Jesus about founding the church. Built on the confession of Christ, against which no power of death can ultimately prevail
verse_connections.text: And I say also unto thee, That thou art Peter, and upon this rock I will build my church; and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it

verse_connections.note: Christ's love for the ἐκκλησία is the model for marriage. He gave Himself entirely — the ἐκκλησία is His bride, purchased with His own blood
verse_connections.text: Husbands, love your wives, even as Christ also loved the church, and gave himself for it

_vocabulary.meaning: to call / to summon / to invite
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: For many are called, but few are chosen
They came to Antioch in Pisidia, and went into the synagogue on the sabbath day

practice.question: What does εὐαγγέλιον (euangelion) mean?

options: Christ died for our sins, was buried, and rose on the third day
practice.question: Paul defines the core content of the euangelion in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. Which best summarises it?

options: Imperial victories or the emperor's birth
practice.question: In the Roman world, euangelion was used for announcements of what?

content.etymology: From eu (good, well) + angelos (messenger). In the Roman world, euangelion was used for the announcement of a great victory in battle or the birth of an emperor. The NT hijacks this imperial term: the real euangelion is not Caesar's victory but Christ's — over sin, death, and hell.
related_words: εὐαγγελίζω (euangelizō, G2097) — to preach the gospel (54×)
content.theological_significance: The content of the gospel: Christ died for our sins, was buried, and rose on the third day (1 Cor 15:3-4). Paul would not let it be distorted — if anyone preaches another euangelion, let him be accursed (Gal 1:8-9). Mark begins his account: The beginning of the euangelion of Jesus Christ, Son of God (Mark 1:1). Our words evangelist, evangelical, and evangelism all derive from this word.

verse_connections.note: Mark launches straight in: ἀρχὴ τοῦ εὐαγγελίου. The entire Gospel of Mark is a euangelion — good news about who Jesus is and what He has done
verse_connections.text: The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God

verse_connections.note: Paul's compact definition of the εὐαγγέλιον: death, burial, resurrection. The three historical facts on which all Christian hope rests
verse_connections.text: For I delivered unto you first of all that which I also received, how that Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures; And that he was buried, and that he rose again the third day

_vocabulary.meaning: to preach the gospel / to announce good news
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: He hath sent me to preach the gospel to the poor
And he gave some, apostles; and some, prophets; and some, evangelists

practice.answer: Fellowship / shared participation / partnership in something together
practice.question: What does κοινωνία (koinōnia) mean?
Acts 2:42 says the early church devoted themselves to four things. Which was one of them?

options: A mystical participation in Christ's death and life
practice.question: In 1 Corinthians 10:16, Paul calls the Lord's Supper the κοινωνία of Christ's blood and body. What does this mean?

content.etymology: From koinos meaning common — the shared, the communal. Koinōnia is much richer than the English word fellowship (which often just means socialising). It means sharing in something together: sharing in Christ, sharing in the Spirit, sharing in suffering, sharing in ministry.
related_words: κοινωνέω (koinōneō, G2841) — to share in, to participate (8×)
content.theological_significance: The early church devoted itself to the apostles' teaching and to koinōnia (Acts 2:42). We have κοινωνία with the Father and with His Son Jesus Christ (1 John 1:3). The Lord's Supper is the κοινωνία of the blood and body of Christ (1 Cor 10:16). Paul calls his partnership with the Philippians a κοινωνία in the gospel (Phil 1:5). It is shared life, not merely shared activities.

verse_connections.note: κοινωνία is one of the four pillars of the early church. Not a programme but a way of life — sharing everything in common under the apostles' teaching
verse_connections.text: And they continued stedfastly in the apostles' doctrine and fellowship, and in breaking of bread, and in prayers

verse_connections.note: κοινωνία begins vertically (with the Father and Son) and flows horizontally (with one another). We cannot have genuine fellowship with each other without first having it with God
verse_connections.text: That which we have seen and heard declare we unto you, that ye also may have fellowship with us: and truly our fellowship is with the Father, and with his Son Jesus Christ

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Are not they which eat of the sacrifices partakers of the altar?

_vocabulary.meaning: to share in / to take part in / to contribute
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Distributing to the necessity of saints; given to hospitality

practice.answer: To baptize / to immerse / to plunge into
practice.question: What does βαπτίζω (baptizō) mean?

options: Total immersion — being completely identified with Christ
practice.question: In secular Greek, βαπτίζω was used for dyeing cloth — plunging it into dye. What does this image suggest about Christian baptism?
Romans 6:3-4 connects baptism to what two events in Christ's life?

content.etymology: From baptō meaning to dip, to plunge, to immerse in liquid. Used in secular Greek for dyeing cloth (the cloth was baptised in the dye — completely immersed). The NT uses it for water baptism and for Spirit baptism (baptism in the Holy Ghost, Acts 1:5).
related_words: βαπτιστής (baptistēs, G910) — the Baptist (12×, title of John)
content.theological_significance: Baptism identifies the believer with Christ's death and resurrection (Rom 6:3-4). The Great Commission commands baptism in the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit (Matt 28:19). Baptism does not save (1 Pet 3:21 clarifies it is an appeal to God, not the washing of the flesh) but it is the public declaration of faith and entry into the visible church community.

verse_connections.note: βαπτίζοντες — baptizing. The Great Commission links disciple-making directly to baptism. The single Name (ὄνομα, singular) of three Persons is the first great trinitarian formula
verse_connections.text: Go ye therefore, and teach all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost

verse_connections.note: Baptism (βαπτίσματος) as death and resurrection — the physical act enacts the spiritual reality. We go under the water as the old self; we rise as the new creation
verse_connections.text: Therefore we are buried with him by baptism into death: that like as Christ was raised up from the dead by the glory of the Father, even so we also should walk in newness of life

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Therefore we are buried with him by baptism into death
In those days came John the Baptist, preaching in the wilderness of Judaea

practice.question: What does ἀπόστολος (apostolos) mean?

options: One sent out with authority and a commission
practice.question: ἀπόστολος comes from apo (from) + stellō (to send). What is the core idea?

options: Every disciple shares in the apostolic sending
practice.question: In John 20:21 Jesus says: As the Father has sent (ἀπέσταλκεν) me, even so send I you. What does this imply?

content.etymology: From apo (from) + stellō (to send). An apostle is one sent out with authority and a commission from another. In the Greek world it could describe a naval expedition or an ambassador. The NT uses it primarily for the Twelve and Paul — those personally commissioned by the risen Christ.
related_words: ἀποστέλλω (apostellō, G649) — to send, to commission (132×)
πέμπω (pempō, G3992) — to send (79×, more general sending)
content.theological_significance: The church is built on the foundation of the ἀπόστολοι and prophets (Eph 2:20). The Twelve were eyewitnesses of the resurrection — a qualification Paul met through the Damascus road encounter (1 Cor 9:1). Jesus Himself is called the Apostle (ἀπόστολον) of our confession (Heb 3:1) — the ultimate Sent One from the Father. Every Christian is sent (John 20:21).

verse_connections.note: ἀποστόλων — apostles. Their teaching (now preserved in Scripture) is the foundation of the church. Christ is the cornerstone that holds it all together
verse_connections.text: And are built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Jesus Christ himself being the chief corner stone

verse_connections.note: Jesus is called the ἀπόστολον — the Sent One. He is both the ultimate apostle (sent by the Father) and the High Priest. The source of all Christian mission
verse_connections.text: Wherefore, holy brethren, partakers of the heavenly calling, consider the Apostle and High Priest of our profession, Christ Jesus

_vocabulary.meaning: to send / to send out with authority
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: As my Father hath sent me, even so send I you
But the Comforter, which is the Holy Ghost, whom the Father will send in my name

vocabulary.title: λόγος — Word
vocabulary.subtitle: G3056 · 330 occurrences · Divine reason made flesh

practice.question: What does λόγος (logos) mean?

options: The abstract rational principle of the universe became a human person
practice.question: John 1:14 says the logos became flesh. What was the significance of this for Greek readers?

options: Jesus is fully God yet distinct from the Father — a trinitarian claim
Jesus became God at His baptism
practice.question: John 1:1 says the logos was with God (relationship) and was God (identity). What does this teach?

content.etymology: From legō meaning to speak or to reason. In Greek philosophy (especially the Stoics and Heraclitus), the logos was the rational principle ordering the universe — the cosmic mind behind reality. Philo of Alexandria, a Jewish philosopher, used logos as an intermediary between God and the world. John takes this entire tradition and declares: that logos is not an abstract principle — He is a Person, and He became flesh.
related_words: λέγω (legō, G3004) — to say, to speak (2354×)
λογίζομαι (logizomai, G3049) — to reckon, to count, to reason (41×)
content.theological_significance: The most theologically loaded word in John's Gospel prologue. The logos was with God (relationship), was God (identity), was in the beginning (eternity), and became flesh (incarnation). The logos creates all things (John 1:3), contains all life (1:4), is the light of all humanity (1:4-5). Jesus IS the logos — the complete, final, personal self-expression of God.

verse_connections.note: Ἐν ἀρχῇ ἦν ὁ λόγος — three statements in one verse: eternal (was in the beginning), relational (with God), and divine (was God). John deliberately echoes Genesis 1:1
verse_connections.text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God

verse_connections.note: ὁ λόγος σὰρξ ἐγένετο — the Word became flesh. The most staggering statement of the Incarnation. The abstract becomes concrete; the eternal becomes temporal; the infinite becomes finite
verse_connections.text: And the Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us, and we beheld his glory

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Jesus said unto them, Verily, verily, I say unto you, Before Abraham was, I am
So then faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God

vocabulary.title: δικαιοσύνη — Righteousness
vocabulary.subtitle: G1343 · 92 occurrences · Being right with God

content.meaning: Righteousness / justness / right standing / covenant faithfulness

practice.question: What does δικαιοσύνη (dikaiosynē) mean?

options: Imputed (credited) to us by faith — Christ's righteousness given to us
practice.question: Philippians 3:9 says Paul wants a righteousness not his own but from God through faith. This righteousness is:

options: The righteousness (δικαιοσύνη) of God in Him
practice.question: 2 Corinthians 5:21 describes the great exchange. God made Christ to be sin so we might become:

content.etymology: From dikaios (just, righteous) and its root dike (right, justice). In Greek, dikaiosynē meant giving each person their due — legal and moral correctness. The NT deepens this: righteousness is relational conformity to God's covenant character, and it is a gift imputed to believers through faith.
related_words: δικαιόω (dikaioō, G1344) — to justify, to declare righteous (39×)
content.theological_significance: The righteousness of God (δικαιοσύνη θεοῦ) is revealed in the gospel (Rom 1:17). This is not God's punitive justice alone but His saving faithfulness — He acts to put things right. God made Christ to be sin for us that we might become the δικαιοσύνη of God in Him (2 Cor 5:21). We receive a righteousness not our own, from God, through faith in Christ (Phil 3:9).

verse_connections.note: δικαιοσύνη θεοῦ — the righteousness of God. Paul's thesis for the entire letter. This saving righteousness is the gift that changes everything
verse_connections.text: For therein is the righteousness of God revealed from faith to faith: as it is written, The just shall live by faith

verse_connections.note: The great exchange: our sin to Christ; His δικαιοσύνη to us. The most compact statement of substitutionary atonement and imputed righteousness in the NT
verse_connections.text: For he hath made him to be sin for us, who knew no sin; that we might be made the righteousness of God in him

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: He maketh his sun to rise on the evil and on the good
Therefore we conclude that a man is justified by faith without the deeds of the law

vocabulary.title: ἀλήθεια — Truth
vocabulary.subtitle: G225 · 109 occurrences · Reality as God sees it

content.meaning: Truth / reality / that which is real and not hidden

practice.answer: Truth / reality / that which is not hidden
practice.question: What does ἀλήθεια (alētheia) mean?

options: Truth is a set of rules
Truth is a Person — Jesus Himself
practice.question: Jesus declares in John 14:6: I am the way, the truth (ἀλήθεια), and the life. What does this mean for our understanding of truth?
John 8:32 promises: and ye shall know the truth (ἀλήθεια), and the truth shall make you ___.

content.etymology: From a (not) + lanthano (to be hidden or forgotten). Alētheia literally means un-hiddenness — that which is not concealed, reality as it actually is. In Platonic philosophy, truth was the realm of ideal Forms — ultimate reality. In the NT, truth is not abstract but personal: Jesus declares I am the truth (John 14:6).
content.theological_significance: Pilate asked What is truth? (John 18:38) while Truth Himself stood before him. Jesus is the alētheia (John 14:6), the Word is alētheia (John 17:17), the Spirit is the Spirit of alētheia (John 16:13). True worship must be in Spirit and in alētheia (John 4:24). The truth sets free (John 8:32). Paul says the church is the pillar and ground of the alētheia (1 Tim 3:15).

verse_connections.note: ἡ ἀλήθεια — the truth (with the definite article: THE truth). Jesus is not one truth among many but THE definitive reality. All other truths are reflections of Him
verse_connections.text: I am the way, the truth, and the life: no man cometh unto the Father, but by me

verse_connections.note: ὁ λόγος ὁ σός ἀλήθειά ἐστιν — Your word is truth. Scripture is ἀλήθεια because it is the word of the God who is alētheia. Sanctification comes through the truth that the Word reveals
verse_connections.text: Sanctify them through thy truth: thy word is truth

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: My Father giveth you the true bread from heaven
He that hath received his testimony hath set to his seal that God is true

practice.question: What does δόξα (doxa) mean?

options: Jesus reflects some of God's glory
Jesus is the exact and direct expression of the Father's own glory
Jesus has less glory than the Father
Jesus received glory only after resurrection
practice.question: In Hebrews 1:3, Jesus is called the brightness/radiance of the Father's δόξα. What does this mean?

options: A word of praise / an expression of glory to God
practice.question: Our English word doxology comes from δόξα + λόγος. What is a doxology?

content.etymology: From dokeō meaning to seem, to appear, to give an impression. In classical Greek, doxa meant opinion or reputation — how one appeared to others. The NT transforms this through the Hebrew concept of kavod (weight, glory) — God's glory is not merely His reputation but His actual weighty, luminous, overwhelming presence and splendour.
related_words: δοξάζω (doxazō, G1392) — to glorify, to honour (61×)
content.theological_significance: God's glory is His self-revelation — the radiant display of His character and presence. The Shekinah cloud was the glory of God made visible. Christ is the radiance (ἀπαύγασμα) of the Father's glory (Heb 1:3). He showed His δόξα at the Transfiguration. We are saved to glorify God (Rom 15:7) and are being transformed into His glory from glory to glory (2 Cor 3:18). The goal of history is the manifestation of God's δόξα filling all things.

verse_connections.note: Jesus prays for the restoration of His pre-incarnate δόξα — the glory He shared with the Father in eternity. This is the strongest statement of Christ's eternal pre-existence in the Gospels
verse_connections.text: And now, O Father, glorify thou me with thine own self with the glory which I had with thee before the world was

verse_connections.note: ἀπὸ δόξης εἰς δόξαν — from glory to glory. The Christian life is a progressive transformation INTO the δόξα of Christ. Beholding produces becoming
verse_connections.text: But we all, with open face beholding as in a glass the glory of the Lord, are changed into the same image from glory to glory

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Herein is my Father glorified, that ye bear much fruit
That he might present it to himself a glorious church, not having spot, or wrinkle

practice.answer: Zōē = spiritual, eternal, God-quality life; bios = biological/physical life
practice.question: What does ζωή (zōē) mean — and how does it differ from bios?
John 10:10 says Jesus came to give ζωή abundantly. What kind of life is this?
1 John 5:12 says He that hath the Son hath ζωή — and he that hath not the Son of God hath not ___. What?

content.etymology: One of two Greek words for life: bios (biological/physical life, origin of biology) and zōē (spiritual, divine-quality life). Zōē is the life that belongs to God Himself and is shared with humanity through Christ. It is not mere survival but the fullness of divine existence.
related_words: αἰώνιος (aiōnios, G166) — eternal, age-lasting (71×, often with ζωή)
ζωοποιέω (zōopoieō, G2227) — to make alive, to give life (11×)
content.theological_significance: Jesus said I am come that they might have ζωή, and that they might have it more abundantly (John 10:10). I am the resurrection and the ζωή (John 11:25). God gave His Son so that everyone who believes should have αἰώνιος ζωή — eternal life (John 3:16). This life begins now (1 John 5:12-13) and extends into eternity. John wrote his Gospel so readers might have ζωή in Christ's name (John 20:31).

verse_connections.note: ζωήν and περισσόν (more abundantly) — Jesus does not offer minimal survival. He offers overflowing, extravagant ζωή that exceeds all we could ask or imagine
verse_connections.text: I am come that they might have life, and that they might have it more abundantly

verse_connections.note: αἰώνιον ζωήν — eternal life. It is not earned or achieved; it is given. And it is entirely located in the Person of Christ — have the Son, have ζωή
verse_connections.text: And this is the record, that God hath given to us eternal life, and this life is in his Son. He that hath the Son hath life

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: I live; yet not I, but Christ liveth in me

_vocabulary.meaning: to make alive / to give life
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: For as the Father raiseth up the dead, and quickeneth them; even so the Son quickeneth whom he will

vocabulary.title: πιστεύω — Believe
vocabulary.subtitle: G4100 · 248 occurrences · The most important NT verb

content.meaning: To believe / to trust / to commit oneself to

practice.answer: To believe / to trust / to commit oneself to
practice.question: What does πιστεύω (pisteuō) mean?

options: Nothing — it is the same as believing about
Active personal entrusting and commitment, not just mental agreement
practice.question: John often writes believe INTO Christ (πιστεύω εἰς). What does the preposition INTO add to the idea of believing?
How many times does πιστεύω appear in John's Gospel alone?

content.etymology: The verb form of πίστις (faith). In Greek, pisteuo means to be persuaded of something, to put confidence in, to entrust oneself to. In the NT it is consistently followed by constructions meaning believe INTO Christ (πιστεύω εἰς) — not merely believe ABOUT Him, but entrust oneself TO Him.
content.theological_significance: The most frequent verb in John's Gospel (98 of its 248 NT occurrences). John specifically wrote his Gospel so that readers might believe (πιστεύω) that Jesus is the Christ and have life (John 20:31). Saving faith is πιστεύω εἰς — believe INTO, a total reliance. The 248 occurrences span every aspect of Christian life: beginning (John 3:16), continuing (John 8:31), and completing (Phil 1:6).

verse_connections.note: πιστεύων εἰς αὐτόν — believing INTO Him. The promise is breathtaking in its inclusivity (whosoever) and its stakes (not perish vs everlasting life)
verse_connections.text: For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life

verse_connections.note: John's purpose statement: πιστεύητε — that you might believe. The entire Gospel is written as an extended invitation to πιστεύω. Every sign, every discourse, every encounter leads here
