verse_connections.text: But these are written, that ye might believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God; and that believing ye might have life through his name

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Lord, I believe; help thou mine unbelief
If thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus

practice.answer: Ginōskō = experiential / relational knowing; oida = intellectual / factual knowing
practice.question: What does γινώσκω (ginōskō) mean — and how does it differ from oida?

options: Relational, experiential knowing — intimate fellowship with God
practice.question: John 17:3 defines eternal life as knowing (γινώσκωσιν) the Father and the Son. What kind of knowing is this?

options: It is the deepest possible intimacy — more than information, a living relationship
It is only available to scholars
practice.question: The Hebrew equivalent of γινώσκω is yada — used for the most intimate human relationship. What does this tell us about knowing God?

content.etymology: Greek has two main words for know: oida (to know as fact, intuitive or intellectual knowledge) and ginōskō (to know through experience, observation, relationship). Ginōskō is the knowing that comes from being in relationship — similar to the Hebrew yada which is used for the intimate knowing of husband and wife.
related_words: οἶδα (oida, G1492) — to know (as fact / intellectually, 318×)
content.theological_significance: John 17:3 defines eternal life: This is life eternal, that they might know (γινώσκωσιν) thee the only true God, and Jesus Christ. Eternal life is not a destination but a relationship — the ongoing knowing of God. Paul's ambition: That I may know (γνῶναι) Him (Phil 3:10). Adam knew (ἔγνω) his wife Eve — same word. The knowledge God seeks is intimate, personal, and relational.

verse_connections.note: γινώσκωσιν — that they might know (ongoing, experiential knowing). Eternal life IS the knowing of God — not just a reward for knowing facts but the relational experience of God Himself
verse_connections.text: And this is life eternal, that they might know thee the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom thou hast sent

verse_connections.note: τοῦ γνῶναι αὐτόν — to know Him. Paul's supreme ambition. After his Damascus road encounter, after years of apostolic ministry, he still longs for deeper γινώσκω of Christ
verse_connections.text: That I may know him, and the power of his resurrection, and the fellowship of his sufferings

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And though I have the gift of prophecy, and understand all mysteries, and all knowledge
That your love may abound yet more and more in knowledge and in all judgment

vocabulary.title: ἀγαπάω — Love
vocabulary.subtitle: G25 · 143 occurrences · Love as a choice

content.meaning: To love (unconditionally, by choice, as God loves)

practice.answer: To love (unconditionally, by choice — the verb form of agapē)
practice.question: What does ἀγαπάω (agapaō) mean?

options: A different word — Peter shifts to phileō (friendship love), possibly unable to claim the full agapaō
Both mean exactly the same thing
practice.question: In John 21, Jesus asks Peter Dost thou love (ἀγαπᾷς) me? Then Peter says Lord, thou knowest that I love (φιλῶ) thee. What is Peter using — the same word or a different one?
The new commandment (John 13:34) is to ἀγαπᾶτε (present imperative: keep on loving) one another. The standard for this love is:

content.etymology: The verb form of ἀγάπη. Like its noun form, this verb was rarely used before the NT and was largely redefined by Christian usage. It emphasises love as a decision and action — not primarily an emotion. God so ἠγάπησεν (loved, verb) the world that He GAVE. The love is demonstrated in the giving.
related_words: φιλέω (phileō, G5368) — to love as a friend, to have affection (25×)
στέργω (stergō) — to love as family (not in NT but related concept)
content.theological_significance: The greatest commandment is to ἀγαπάω God and neighbour (Matt 22:37-39). The new commandment is to ἀγαπάω one another as Christ has loved us (John 13:34). The verb tense matters: ἀγαπάτε (present imperative) = keep on loving, love continuously. After the resurrection, Jesus asked Peter three times Dost thou love me (ἀγαπᾷς με vs φιλεῖς με) — the famous exchange exploring the nature of Peter's love.

verse_connections.note: ἀγαπᾶτε — keep on loving. The standard is Christ's own love (ἠγάπησα). This is not the law's standard (love your neighbour as yourself) but an impossibly higher one: as I have loved you
verse_connections.text: A new commandment I give unto you, That ye love one another; as I have loved you, that ye also love one another

verse_connections.note: ἀγάπην ἑαυτοῦ — His own love. God's ἀγαπάω is demonstrated in the most extreme circumstance: loving the unlovable, dying for the ungrateful. This is the definition of agapē in action
verse_connections.text: But God commendeth his love toward us, in that, while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Beloved, let us love one another: for love is of God

_vocabulary.meaning: to love as a friend / to have warm affection for
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Then said the Jews, Behold how he loved him

vocabulary.title: μετανοέω — Repent
vocabulary.subtitle: G3340 · 34 occurrences · Change of mind and direction

content.meaning: To repent / to change one's mind / to turn around

practice.answer: To repent — to change one's mind and direction, to turn toward God
practice.question: What does μετανοέω (metanoeō) mean?

options: The mind / thinking — leading to a change of direction
practice.question: μετανοέω comes from meta (change) + noeō (to think). What part of the person does repentance primarily involve?
What was the first word of both John the Baptist's and Jesus' public preaching?

content.etymology: From meta (change, after) + noeō (to think, from nous, mind). Repentance is literally a change of mind — a fundamental reorientation of thinking that leads to a change of direction. It is not merely feeling sorry (that is sorrow, lypē) but a decisive mental and volitional turn toward God and away from sin.
related_words: μεταμέλομαι (metamelomai, G3338) — to regret, to feel remorse (6×, different from true repentance)
content.theological_significance: The first word of both John the Baptist's preaching and Jesus' public ministry: μετανοεῖτε (Matt 3:2, 4:17). The first sermon of the church at Pentecost calls for repentance (Acts 2:38). True repentance involves three elements: intellectual (changing one's understanding of sin and God), emotional (genuine sorrow, 2 Cor 7:10), and volitional (turning around — demonstrated by changed behaviour). God's kindness leads to repentance (Rom 2:4).

verse_connections.note: μετανοεῖτε — repent (present imperative: keep repenting, make it a lifestyle). Jesus begins His public ministry with this call. Repentance is the gateway to the kingdom
verse_connections.text: From that time Jesus began to preach, and to say, Repent: for the kingdom of heaven is at hand

verse_connections.note: μετανοήσατε — repent (aorist imperative: do it now, decisively). The first call of the church after Pentecost. μετανοέω leads to βαπτίζω leads to receiving the πνεῦμα
verse_connections.text: Then Peter said unto them, Repent, and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the remission of sins, and ye shall receive the gift of the Holy Ghost

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Bring forth therefore fruits meet for repentance

_vocabulary.meaning: to regret / to feel remorse (without necessarily changing course)
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Then Judas, which had betrayed him, when he saw that he was condemned, repented himself

vocabulary.title: δοξάζω — Glorify
vocabulary.subtitle: G1392 · 61 occurrences · Giving weight to God

content.meaning: To glorify / to honour / to magnify / to give praise

practice.answer: To glorify / to honour God / to ascribe glory to Him
practice.question: What does δοξάζω (doxazō) mean?

options: Every ordinary action can and should glorify God
Glorifying God requires special spiritual gifts
practice.question: 1 Corinthians 10:31 says: Whether therefore ye eat, or drink, or whatsoever ye do, do all to the δόξα of God. What does this mean practically?

options: Finishing the work the Father gave Him
practice.question: In John 17:4, Jesus says He glorified the Father by doing what?

content.etymology: The verb form of δόξα (glory). To glorify God means to acknowledge His true weight and worth — to ascribe to Him the glory due His name. It is the proper response of the creature to the Creator. The opposite is to rob God of His δόξα or to give it to something else (Rom 1:21-23).
related_words: ἐνδοξάζω (endoxazō, G1740) — to glorify in (2 Thess 1:10,12)
συνδοξάζω (syndoxazō, G4888) — to be glorified with (Rom 8:17)
content.theological_significance: The chief end of human existence: to glorify God and enjoy Him forever (Westminster Catechism paraphrase of 1 Cor 10:31). Jesus glorified the Father through His works (John 17:4) and the Father glorified the Son through resurrection (John 13:31-32). We glorify God by bearing fruit (John 15:8), by our bodies (1 Cor 6:20), and by every word and action (1 Cor 10:31). The Holy Spirit glorifies the Son (John 16:14).

verse_connections.note: δεδόξακά σε — I have glorified You. Jesus' life was a complete act of δοξάζω — every moment, every healing, every word pointed to the Father's glory. This is the model for our lives
verse_connections.text: I have glorified thee on the earth: I have finished the work which thou gavest me to do

verse_connections.note: δοξάζηται ὁ θεός — that God may be glorified. Ministry, teaching, serving — all are channels of δοξάζω. The test of every activity is: does this make God look glorious?
verse_connections.text: If any man speak, let him speak as the oracles of God; if any man minister, let him do it as of the ability which God giveth: that God in all things may be glorified through Jesus Christ

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: And we beheld his glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father
Let nothing be done through strife or vainglory

practice.answer: Coming / royal arrival / presence — the Second Coming of Christ
practice.question: What does παρουσία (parousia) mean?

options: It shows the second coming is unexpected
It shows the second coming is a royal event with cosmic preparation and a procession to meet the King
It shows the second coming is only for Jews
It shows the second coming is invisible
practice.question: In the Hellenistic world, parousia was used for the official visit of a king. How does this background enrich Matthew 24 and 1 Thessalonians 4?

options: The dead are in heaven already
At the παρουσία, the dead in Christ rise first, then the living are caught up together with them
practice.question: 1 Thessalonians 4:15-17 uses παρουσία to comfort those grieving. What is the comfort?

content.etymology: From para (beside, alongside) + ousia (being, essence — from eimi, to be). Literally: the being-alongside, the presence. In the Hellenistic world, parousia was the technical term for the official visit of a king or emperor to a city. The city would prepare in advance, go out to meet him, and escort him back. The NT uses exactly this imagery for Christ's return.
content.theological_significance: The Second Coming of Christ is His royal παρουσία — His official royal visit to earth as King. Used 24 times in the NT, primarily by Paul and in Matthew 24. The four features of the parousia: suddenness (1 Thess 5:2), glory (Matt 24:27), gathering of the elect (Matt 24:31), and judgment (2 Thess 2:8). Paul comforts bereaved believers by pointing to the παρουσία (1 Thess 4:15-17).

verse_connections.note: παρουσία — the coming. Sudden, universal, unmistakable — like lightning across the whole sky. No one will miss the παρουσία of the Son of Man
verse_connections.text: For as the lightning cometh out of the east, and shineth even unto the west; so shall also the coming of the Son of man be

verse_connections.note: παρουσίαν τοῦ κυρίου — the royal arrival of the Lord. Paul's great comfort to grieving believers: death does not end the story; the παρουσία completes it
verse_connections.text: For this we say unto you by the word of the Lord, that we which are alive and remain unto the coming of the Lord shall not prevent them which are asleep

practice.answer: Revelation / unveiling — the removing of a covering to reveal what was hidden
practice.question: What does ἀποκάλυψις (apokalypsis) mean?

options: Jesus Christ Himself — His person, His reign, His victory
practice.question: The book of Revelation is called ἀποκάλυψις Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (Rev 1:1). It is primarily a revelation OF what?

options: The glorious unveiling of believers in resurrection glory — which will trigger the renewal of all creation
practice.question: Romans 8:19 says creation waits for the ἀποκάλυψις of the sons of God. What is this?

content.etymology: From apo (away from) + kalyptō (to cover, to veil). Apokalypsis is the removing of a veil — an unveiling of what was previously hidden. The last book of the Bible takes its name from this word (The Revelation of John) because it is the great unveiling of what God is doing in history.
related_words: ἀποκαλύπτω (apokalyptō, G601) — to reveal, to unveil (26×)
καλύπτω (kalyptō, G2572) — to cover, to veil (8×)
φανερόω (phaneroō, G5319) — to make visible, to manifest (49×)
content.theological_significance: The book of Revelation opens: The ἀποκάλυψις of Jesus Christ (Rev 1:1) — it is a revelation OF Christ, not merely about Him. Paul speaks of the ἀποκάλυψις of the righteous judgment of God (Rom 2:5) and of the sons of God (Rom 8:19). The gospel itself is a divine ἀποκάλυψις — God pulling back the veil to show His saving purpose hidden from ages (Eph 3:3-5).

verse_connections.note: ἀποκάλυψις Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ — the great unveiling. The entire book peels back the curtain between visible and invisible reality to show Christ reigning over all of history
verse_connections.text: The Revelation of Jesus Christ, which God gave unto him, to shew unto his servants things which must shortly come to pass

verse_connections.note: ἀποκάλυψιν τῶν υἱῶν τοῦ θεοῦ — the unveiling of God's sons. All creation holds its breath for the moment when believers are glorified — a cosmic ἀποκάλυψις that renews everything
verse_connections.text: For the earnest expectation of the creature waiteth for the manifestation of the sons of God

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: To reveal his Son in me, that I might preach him among the heathen

_vocabulary.meaning: to make visible / to manifest / to disclose
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: When Christ, who is our life, shall appear, then shall ye also appear with him in glory

vocabulary.title: βασιλεία — Kingdom
vocabulary.subtitle: G932 · 162 occurrences · The reign of God

practice.question: What does βασιλεία (basileia) mean?

options: Luke 17:21 (the kingdom of God is within/among you)
Matthew 25:34 (come ye blessed, inherit the kingdom)
Revelation 11:15 (the kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord)
practice.question: Jesus said the βασιλεία of God is both present and future. Which verse shows it is PRESENT?

options: Righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit
practice.question: Romans 14:17 defines the βασιλεία of God as consisting of what three things?

content.etymology: From basileus meaning king. Basileia is both the realm of a king and the act of his reigning — not just a territory but a dynamic rule. The Kingdom of God is wherever and whenever God's sovereign will is being done.
related_words: βασιλεύω (basileuō, G936) — to reign, to be king (21×)
content.theological_significance: The central theme of Jesus' preaching. The Synoptic Gospels record kingdom language constantly. The Kingdom is both present (Luke 17:21, the kingdom is within/among you) and future (Matt 6:10, thy kingdom come). It is entered through new birth (John 3:3,5), through repentance and faith (Mark 1:15), and through the Spirit (Rom 14:17). The Sermon on the Mount is the constitution of the Kingdom. The Lord's Prayer is the Kingdom prayer. Revelation closes with the Kingdom fully arrived.

verse_connections.note: βασιλεία σου — Your kingdom. The Lord's Prayer is fundamentally a Kingdom prayer. God's reign coming means His will being done — the two are inseparable
verse_connections.text: Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done in earth, as it is in heaven

verse_connections.note: ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ θεοῦ — the Kingdom of God. Jesus' first public sermon in Mark. Repentance and faith are the entry requirements. The Kingdom has arrived in the Person of the King
verse_connections.text: The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand: repent ye, and believe the gospel

_vocabulary.example_verse_text: These shall make war with the Lamb, and the Lamb shall overcome them: for he is Lord of lords, and King of kings

_vocabulary.meaning: to reign / to be king / to rule
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: They which receive abundance of grace and of the gift of righteousness shall reign in life by one, Jesus Christ

practice.question: What does κρίσις (krisis) mean?
John 5:22 says the Father has committed all judgment (κρίσιν) to the Son. What will the standard of that judgment be?

options: Reincarnation and purgatory (no second chance after death)
practice.question: Hebrews 9:27 says it is appointed for humans to die once, then comes κρίσις. What does this deny?

content.etymology: From krinō meaning to separate, to distinguish, to judge, to decide. Krisis is the act of discernment — separating and evaluating. Our word crisis comes directly from Greek (a crisis is a turning point requiring a decisive judgment). In the NT, krisis is primarily God's final evaluation of all human actions and choices.
related_words: κρίνω (krinō, G2919) — to judge, to evaluate (114×)
κατακρίνω (katakrinō, G2632) — to condemn, to judge against (18×)
content.theological_significance: It is appointed for humans to die once, and after that comes krisis (Heb 9:27). The Father has committed all krisis to the Son (John 5:22). The standard of krisis is the word of Christ — His λόγος will judge at the last day (John 12:48). The outcome of krisis is eternal life or second death. Believers stand before the Bema Seat (judgment seat) for rewards (2 Cor 5:10); unbelievers face the Great White Throne (Rev 20:11-15).

verse_connections.note: πᾶσαν τὴν κρίσιν — all the judgment. The authority of final evaluation rests entirely with Christ. Every human being will stand before the One they either received or rejected
verse_connections.text: For the Father judgeth no man, but hath committed all judgment unto the Son

verse_connections.note: κρίσις — after death comes judgment. The most sobering κρίσις verse: one life, one death, one judgment. No cycle of reincarnation, no second chance. The urgency of the gospel rests on this fact
verse_connections.text: And as it is appointed unto men once to die, but after this the judgment

_vocabulary.meaning: to judge / to evaluate / to condemn
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: He that rejecteth me, and receiveth not my words, hath one that judgeth him
God, sending his own Son in the likeness of sinful flesh, and for sin, condemned sin in the flesh

content.meaning: Eternal / everlasting / age-lasting / of the Age to Come

practice.answer: Eternal / everlasting / belonging to the Age to Come
practice.question: What does αἰώνιος (aiōnios) mean?

options: Eternal life is only a future promise
Eternal life begins NOW at the moment of belief
Eternal life is conditional on continued obedience
practice.question: John 5:24 says the one who believes HAS (present tense) eternal life. What does the present tense tell us?

options: Going to heaven when you die
Knowing God the Father and Jesus Christ whom He sent
practice.question: John 17:3 defines αἰώνιος ζωή as what?

content.etymology: From aiōn meaning age or era (not just time in general, but a significant epoch). Aiōnios describes that which belongs to or partakes of the Age to Come — the coming eternal order. In John's Gospel especially, eternal life is not just unending duration but a quality of life belonging to the divine realm.
related_words: ζωή αἰώνιος — eternal life (the phrase, most important usage)
content.theological_significance: The most important modifier in the NT: αἰώνιος ζωή (eternal life, 43 of its 71 occurrences). This life begins the moment of belief (John 5:24: has — present tense — eternal life) and never ends. Jesus defines it relationally: knowing God and Christ (John 17:3). Αἰώνιος also describes eternal punishment (Matt 25:46), eternal judgment (Heb 6:2), and eternal glory (2 Cor 4:17). The Age to Come has already broken into the present through Christ.

verse_connections.note: ζωὴν αἰώνιον — everlasting life. The most famous promise in Scripture hinges on αἰώνιος. Not just endless life but the rich, full, divine-quality life that belongs to the Age to Come
verse_connections.text: That whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life

verse_connections.note: κόλασιν αἰώνιον... ζωὴν αἰώνιον — the same adjective αἰώνιος applies to both outcomes. The same eternity awaits all — either in blessing or in judgment. The word makes both permanent.
verse_connections.text: And these shall go away into everlasting punishment: but the righteous into life eternal

_vocabulary.meaning: age / era / eternity / the present world order
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Lo, I am with you always, even unto the end of the world

_vocabulary.meaning: the present age (contrasted with the Age to Come)
_vocabulary.example_verse_text: Who gave himself for our sins, that he might deliver us from this present evil world
